Input.Unnamed: 76: Female patients aged 40 years or older with newly diagnosed, screen-detected, grade 1 or 2, hormone receptor-positive (estrogen and/or progesterone receptor ≥10% staining), HER2-negative ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) without evidence of invasive or microinvasive disease. Input.Unnamed: 18: HER2-targeted antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) with topoisomerase I inhibitor payload
Input.Unnamed: 21: Site-specific, construct-homogeneous ADC (DAR ~2) using EuCODE technology
Input.Unnamed: 25: Autologous tumor infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy + anti-PD-1 antibody
Input.Unnamed: 27: Therapeutic cancer vaccine (T-win platform) targeting IDO1 and PD-L1 pathways + anti-PD-1
Input.Unnamed: 28: PARP inhibitor (niraparib) + androgen synthesis inhibitor (abiraterone) + corticosteroid
Input.Unnamed: 31: RNA polymerase II inhibitor (lurbinectedin) + anti-PD-L1 antibody (atezolizumab)
Input.Unnamed: 33: Antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting HER2 with a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload, designed to selectively deliver cytotoxic drug to tumor cells expressing HER2 antigen.
Input.Unnamed: 34: Selective PI3Kα inhibitor that blocks the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway and promotes degradation of mutated p110α protein
Input.Unnamed: 35: CD20×CD3 bispecific antibody that binds CD20 on B-cells and CD3 on T-cells, leading to T-cell activation and B-cell lysis
Input.Unnamed: 36: Targeted radiopharmaceutical therapy - radiolabeled somatostatin analog delivers beta radiation to somatostatin receptor-positive (SSTR+) tumors, inducing DNA damage and cell death through internalization of the ligand-receptor complex and subsequent ionizing radiation effects.
Input.Unnamed: 37: Nectin-4 antibody-drug conjugate (enfortumab vedotin) binds to Nectin-4 on tumor cells, is internalized, and releases the microtubule-disrupting agent MMAE, causing cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis; combined with a PD-1 inhibitor (pembrolizumab) which blocks PD-1 on T cells to enhance anti-tumor immune response
Input.Unnamed: 38: Bispecific T-cell engager targeting DLL3 and CD3, which binds DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, bringing them into close proximity to form an immunological synapse, resulting in T-cell activation, release of inflammatory cytokines (such as perforin and granzyme B), and lysis of DLL3-expressing tumor cells.
Input.Unnamed: 39: Oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that binds to the estrogen receptor, induces an inactive conformation, and promotes proteasome-mediated degradation of the receptor protein, thereby inhibiting estrogen-dependent and independent ER signaling.
Input.Unnamed: 40: Oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD)
Input.Unnamed: 41: Oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor that inhibits multiple receptor tyrosine kinases including VEGFR2, MET, AXL, and RET, thereby blocking downstream signaling pathways involved in tumor cell proliferation, angiogenesis, migration, invasion, and drug resistance.
Input.Unnamed: 42: Inavolisib is a selective PI3Kα inhibitor that blocks the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway by inhibiting the catalytic activity of the p110α subunit, thereby reducing PIP3 production and downstream signaling involved in cell growth, survival, and metabolism.
Input.Unnamed: 43: BCMA×CD3 bispecific antibody that bridges BCMA on myeloma cells with CD3 on T cells to induce T-cell activation and targeted lysis of myeloma cells
Input.Unnamed: 44: Non-covalent (reversible) BTK inhibitor that binds to Bruton's tyrosine kinase and inhibits its activity, blocking B-cell receptor signaling and downstream survival pathways in malignant B-cells
Input.Unnamed: 45: Oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that binds to and promotes degradation of the estrogen receptor, combined with an mTOR inhibitor that blocks the mTOR signaling pathway, thereby inhibiting cell proliferation and survival in cancer cells
Input.Unnamed: 46: Combined inhibition of BRAF and EGFR blocks the MAPK signaling pathway at multiple points, preventing adaptive feedback reactivation and maintaining suppression of cell proliferation signals in BRAF V600E-mutant colorectal cancer.
Input.Unnamed: 47: ONC201 is a dopamine receptor D2 (DRD2) antagonist and mitochondrial ClpP protease agonist. It induces the integrated stress response (ISR) and tumor necrosis factor-related apoptosis-inducing ligand (TRAIL) pathways, leading to upregulation of death receptor 5 (DR5), inhibition of the AKT/mTOR/S6 pathways, and activation of apoptosis and cell cycle arrest. Its cytotoxic effects are mediated through both DRD2 antagonism and ClpP activation, with ClpP activation being essential for its antitumor activity.
Input.Unnamed: 48: CD20×CD3 bispecific antibody that binds CD20 on B-cells and CD3 on T-cells, leading to T-cell activation and B-cell lysis
Input.Unnamed: 49: PARP inhibitor that blocks PARP catalytic activity and traps PARP on DNA, preventing DNA repair and causing synthetic lethality in BRCA-deficient cells
Input.Unnamed: 50: PD-L1 inhibitor (immune checkpoint inhibitor) that blocks PD-L1/PD-1 interaction to restore T cell-mediated anti-tumor immune responses
Input.Unnamed: 51: Dual immune checkpoint blockade: nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor) and ipilimumab (CTLA-4 inhibitor) work synergistically to release T cell inhibition and enhance antitumor immune responses
Input.Unnamed: 52: Dual checkpoint inhibition targeting both LAG-3 and PD-1: fianlimab blocks LAG-3, preventing its interaction with MHC class II and other ligands, while cemiplimab blocks PD-1, preventing its interaction with PD-L1 and PD-L2. This dual blockade reactivates exhausted T cells and enhances anti-tumor immune responses by overcoming complementary inhibitory pathways.
Input.Unnamed: 53: Pembrolizumab is a monoclonal antibody that binds to the PD-1 receptor on T cells, blocking its interaction with PD-L1 and PD-L2 ligands. This blockade prevents the inhibition of T cell activation and restores the immune response against tumor cells.
Input.Unnamed: 54: PD-1 inhibitor: blocks the interaction between PD-1 on T cells and PD-L1 on tumor cells, preventing immune suppression and restoring T cell-mediated anti-tumor activity
Input.Unnamed: 55: ROR1-targeting antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) that binds to ROR1 on tumor cells, is internalized, and releases a cytotoxic payload (MMAE) to induce tumor cell death
Input.Unnamed: 56: Amivantamab: Bispecific antibody targeting EGFR and MET with immune cell-directing activity, ligand blocking, and receptor degradation; Lazertinib: Third-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI)
Input.Unnamed: 57: PD-L1 inhibitor that blocks the interaction between PD-L1 on tumor cells and PD-1 on T cells, thereby preventing immune suppression and restoring T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity
Input.Unnamed: 58: Cancer vaccine that stimulates immune system to recognize alpha-lactalbumin antigen, leading to antigen presentation by dendritic cells and activation of T cells for anti-tumor immune response
Input.Unnamed: 59: BCL2 inhibitor that selectively binds to BCL-2, displacing pro-apoptotic proteins and triggering apoptosis in cancer cells
Input.Unnamed: 60: Dendritic cell vaccine with synthetic peptides targeting tumor-associated antigens, designed to activate dendritic cells and induce antigen-specific T-cell responses against tumor cells
Input.Unnamed: 61: Selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that binds to the estrogen receptor, induces a conformational change, and promotes its degradation via the ubiquitin-proteasome system, thereby inhibiting estrogen signaling and tumor growth.
Input.Unnamed: 62: PARP inhibitor that blocks DNA repair and traps PARP proteins on DNA, causing synthetic lethality in cells with homologous recombination deficiency (e.g., BRCA1/2 mutations)
Input.Unnamed: 63: KRAS G12C inhibitors covalently bind to the mutated cysteine residue at position 12 of KRAS, trapping the protein in an inactive GDP-bound state and preventing nucleotide exchange to the active GTP-bound form, thereby inhibiting downstream oncogenic signaling.
Input.Unnamed: 64: TROP2-targeted antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) with a novel pyrimidine-thiol linker conjugated to a belotecan-derivative topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DAR 7.4); the hydrolytically sensitive linker permits both extracellular pH-sensitive cleavage and intracellular enzymatic cleavage to release the membrane-permeable payload, enabling a bystander effect
Input.Unnamed: 65: PD-1 inhibitor (monoclonal antibody that blocks PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T cell-mediated anti-tumor immune responses)
Input.Unnamed: 66: B7-H3-directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) comprising a humanized anti-B7-H3 IgG1 monoclonal antibody attached to topoisomerase I inhibitor payloads (exatecan derivative, DXd) via tetrapeptide-based cleavable linkers, designed to enhance selective tumor cell death and reduce systemic exposure
Input.Unnamed: 67: Antibody-drug conjugate targeting TROP2 with topoisomerase I inhibitor payload
Input.Unnamed: 68: Oral, reversible tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) that potently inhibits mutant HER2 (including HER2 exon 20 insertions and HER2 point mutations) and EGFR, with high selectivity for mutant vs wild-type EGFR
Input.Unnamed: 69: Bispecific antibody-drug conjugate targeting EGFR and HER3, binding to both receptors on tumor cells, internalizing via endocytosis, and releasing a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload to induce tumor cell death.
Input.Unnamed: 70: HER2-directed antibody-drug conjugate consisting of trastuzumab linked to a topoisomerase I inhibitor (deruxtecan) via a stable, tumor-selective cleavable linker. The antibody binds HER2 on tumor cells, leading to internalization, lysosomal degradation, and release of the cytotoxic payload, which induces DNA damage and cell death. The drug also exhibits immune-boosting effects such as antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC).
Input.Unnamed: 71: ATR kinase inhibitor (ceralasertib) disrupts DNA damage response and activates innate immune pathways (cGAS-STING), combined with PD-L1 inhibitor (durvalumab) to restore T cell function and enhance antitumor immunity
Input.Unnamed: 72: Anti-CTLA-4 monoclonal antibody that blocks the inhibitory signal resulting from CTLA-4 binding to CD80/86, leading to prolongation and enhancement of T-cell activation and expansion. This results in increased activation and expansion of tumor antigen-specific cytotoxic T cells (CTLs) and may reduce the suppressive activity of regulatory T cells (Tregs) in the tumor microenvironment.
Input.Unnamed: 73: EGFR-TKI inhibits EGFR signaling pathways, blocking tumor cell proliferation and survival, while chemotherapy (platinum-pemetrexed) induces DNA damage and cell death in rapidly dividing cancer cells. The combination enhances anti-tumor efficacy through synergistic effects, potentially overcoming or delaying resistance.
Input.Unnamed: 74: Crizotinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that binds to the ATP-binding pocket of the abnormal ALK protein, blocking its kinase activity and thereby inhibiting downstream signaling pathways involved in cell proliferation and survival.
Input.Unnamed: 75: Therapeutic cancer vaccine targeting α-lactalbumin, a lactation-associated protein typically expressed only in breast tissue during lactation but re-expressed in many forms of breast cancer including TNBC. The vaccine activates the immune system to direct cytotoxic T cells against tumor cells expressing α-lactalbumin, providing preemptive immune protection against emerging breast tumors. Induces α-lactalbumin-specific T cell responses with interferon gamma and interleukin-17 production.
Input.Unnamed: 76: N/A - comparing management strategies (active monitoring vs. surgery), not drug mechanisms

Input.Unnamed: 22: SOC: mFOLFOX6, FOLFOXIRI, or CAPOX ± bevacizumab
Input.Unnamed: 33: For HER2-low metastatic breast cancer after prior therapy, standard of care is physician's choice chemotherapy (capecitabine, eribulin, gemcitabine, or taxanes). For HER2-positive disease, first-line standard is taxane plus trastuzumab and pertuzumab.
Input.Unnamed: 34: CDK4/6 inhibitor combined with endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitor or fulvestrant) for first-line treatment; alpelisib plus fulvestrant for PIK3CA-mutated patients after progression on endocrine therapy
Input.Unnamed: 35: For relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma, standard of care includes rituximab plus lenalidomide (R2), bendamustine plus rituximab, CHOP or CVP-based chemoimmunotherapy, or other anti-CD20 antibody-based regimens. Treatment selection depends on prior therapies, response duration, and patient factors.
Input.Unnamed: 36: Somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) for symptom control and disease stabilization; 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) for eligible patients with progressive disease; everolimus or sunitinib for progressive disease
Input.Unnamed: 37: Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy + radical cystectomy (for cisplatin-eligible); immediate radical cystectomy (for cisplatin-ineligible)
Input.Unnamed: 38: Second-line chemotherapy options: topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin (investigator's choice)
Input.Unnamed: 39: For metastatic HR+ breast cancer, standard of care is endocrine therapy, typically aromatase inhibitors (AIs) or fulvestrant, often combined with CDK4/6 inhibitors in the first-line setting. For early-stage HR+ breast cancer, standard of care is 5-10 years of endocrine therapy (tamoxifen, AIs, or sequential therapy) after surgery.
Input.Unnamed: 40: Adjuvant endocrine therapy with aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane) for postmenopausal women or tamoxifen for premenopausal women, typically for 5-10 years
Input.Unnamed: 41: For previously treated, progressive advanced neuroendocrine tumors (pancreatic and extra-pancreatic), the standard of care is typically a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) such as sunitinib or everolimus for pancreatic NETs, or everolimus for extra-pancreatic NETs. For patients who have progressed on prior TKIs, options may include peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) with lutetium Lu 177 dotatate, or chemotherapy regimens such as capecitabine and temozolomide. Cabozantinib is now an approved option for second-line or later therapy.
Input.Unnamed: 42: CDK4/6 inhibitors combined with endocrine therapy (such as aromatase inhibitors or fulvestrant) is the standard first-line treatment for hormone receptor-positive (HR+), HER2-negative (HER2-) advanced or metastatic breast cancer. For early breast cancer, CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib, ribociclib) are approved in specific high-risk populations in combination with endocrine therapy.
Input.Unnamed: 43: For relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma after 1-4 prior lines, standard regimens include combinations of immunomodulatory drugs (lenalidomide, pomalidomide), proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, carfilzomib), anti-CD38 antibodies (daratumumab, isatuximab), and corticosteroids. Increasingly, bispecific antibodies (teclistamab, elranatamab) and CAR-T therapies (cilta-cel, ide-cel) are used in later lines.
Input.Unnamed: 44: BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) as monotherapy or venetoclax-based combinations for most patients; chemoimmunotherapy (bendamustine-rituximab, FCR) for select fit patients without high-risk features
Input.Unnamed: 45: CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitor or fulvestrant) in 1st-line; endocrine therapy (fulvestrant, aromatase inhibitor, or exemestane) plus everolimus in 2nd-line post-CDK4/6 inhibitor progression
Input.Unnamed: 46: For patients with metastatic colorectal cancer, the current standard of care is mFOLFOX6 or FOLFIRI chemotherapy combined with either bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) or cetuximab/panitumumab (anti-EGFR) for those with RAS wild-type tumors. For BRAF V600E-mutated tumors, encorafenib + cetuximab is now FDA-approved in the first-line setting.
Input.Unnamed: 47: For newly diagnosed multiple myeloma, the current standard of care is a four-drug combination regimen such as daratumumab plus carfilzomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone (DKRd), especially for transplant-eligible patients. For standard- and intermediate-risk patients, bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone (VRd) remains a standard triplet induction. Induction therapy is followed by autologous stem cell transplant (if eligible) and maintenance therapy, typically with lenalidomide.
Input.Unnamed: 48: R-CHOP (rituximab plus cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) for first-line treatment of DLBCL
Input.Unnamed: 49: Chemotherapy, surgery, and radiation therapy as indicated; olaparib is now approved as adjuvant therapy for this population based on OlympiA trial results
Input.Unnamed: 50: mFOLFOX6 alone (historical standard); atezolizumab + mFOLFOX6 (new standard of care based on ATOMIC trial results, ASCO 2025)
Input.Unnamed: 51: First-line: Pembrolizumab monotherapy or nivolumab + ipilimumab (both FDA-approved 2025); chemotherapy (FOLFOX/FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab/cetuximab) is alternative option
Input.Unnamed: 52: For previously untreated patients with advanced or metastatic melanoma, the current standard of care is combination immunotherapy with a PD-1 inhibitor (e.g., pembrolizumab or nivolumab) plus a CTLA-4 inhibitor (ipilimumab) or a PD-1/LAG-3 inhibitor combination (e.g., relatlimab + nivolumab, Opdualag). For BRAF V600 mutation-positive patients, targeted therapy with BRAF/MEK inhibitors is also an option.
Input.Unnamed: 53: Anthracycline and taxane-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy, often with platinum agents (carboplatin), for high-risk early-stage TNBC
Input.Unnamed: 54: FOLFOX or FOLFIRI chemotherapy ± bevacizumab or cetuximab (historical standard); pembrolizumab is now standard of care for MSI-H/dMMR mCRC
Input.Unnamed: 55: R-CHOP (rituximab plus cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) for most patients with previously untreated DLBCL
Input.Unnamed: 56: Osimertinib (third-generation EGFR TKI) as first-line treatment for EGFR-mutated (exon 19del or L858R) advanced NSCLC
Input.Unnamed: 57: Cisplatin-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (typically gemcitabine + cisplatin) followed by radical cystectomy
Input.Unnamed: 58: Adjuvant chemotherapy (anthracycline and taxane-based regimens) for high-risk TNBC; pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy in neoadjuvant setting for PD-L1 positive tumors
Input.Unnamed: 59: For relapsed/refractory mantle cell lymphoma after BTK inhibitor and anti-CD20 therapy, standard options include alternative BTK inhibitors (if not previously used), chemotherapy regimens (bendamustine, cytarabine-based), venetoclax-based combinations, and CAR-T cell therapy (brexucabtagene autoleucel) in eligible patients
Input.Unnamed: 60: Maximum safe surgical resection followed by concurrent radiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) and temozolomide (75 mg/m²/day for 6 weeks), then adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m²/day for 5 days every 28 days for up to 6 cycles).
Input.Unnamed: 61: CDK4/6 inhibitors combined with endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitors or fulvestrant) are the standard first-line treatment for hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative advanced or metastatic breast cancer, and are increasingly used in the adjuvant setting for high-risk early breast cancer.
Input.Unnamed: 62: Surgery and chemotherapy (neoadjuvant or adjuvant), with endocrine therapy for hormone receptor-positive disease
Input.Unnamed: 63: For patients with KRAS G12C-mutated locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC who have received prior chemotherapy and immunotherapy, KRAS G12C inhibitors such as sotorasib or adagrasib are approved treatment options. Other options may include docetaxel or other chemotherapy regimens.
Input.Unnamed: 64: For patients with advanced or metastatic nonsquamous NSCLC harboring EGFR mutations who have progressed following a tyrosine kinase inhibitor and platinum-based chemotherapy, the current standard of care includes chemotherapy (docetaxel or pemetrexed) or targeted therapy with amivantamab (for EGFR exon20 insertion mutations) or mobocertinib (for EGFR exon20 insertion mutations)
Input.Unnamed: 65: Traditional standard: neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by surgery. Emerging practice: neoadjuvant PD-1 inhibitor monotherapy (based on recent breakthrough data showing 100% clinical complete response rates in dMMR/MSI-H rectal cancer)
Input.Unnamed: 66: For patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer who have progressed after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, standard options include topotecan, lurbinectedin, or tarlatamab (approved May 2024). Immunotherapy rechallenge may be considered if not used in first-line. Clinical trial participation is strongly encouraged given limited efficacy of available options.
Input.Unnamed: 67: For 2nd-line ES-SCLC, current standard of care options include topotecan, lurbinectedin (approved 2020), or rechallenge with platinum-based chemotherapy. Tarlatamab (DLL3 bispecific) was recently approved in 2024 for relapsed ES-SCLC.
Input.Unnamed: 68: Platinum-based chemotherapy; trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) is approved for HER2-mutant NSCLC
Input.Unnamed: 69: For first-line EGFR-mutated NSCLC, the current standard of care is third-generation EGFR TKI monotherapy (osimertinib) or first/second-generation EGFR TKIs (erlotinib, gefitinib, afatinib)
Input.Unnamed: 70: For first-line treatment of HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer, the standard of care is the combination of taxane chemotherapy (e.g., paclitaxel or docetaxel), trastuzumab, and pertuzumab (THP regimen). However, recent data from DESTINY-Breast09 show that trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) plus pertuzumab is superior to THP and is likely to become the new standard of care.
Input.Unnamed: 71: Docetaxel chemotherapy for patients with advanced NSCLC who have progressed on anti-PD-(L)1 therapy and platinum-based chemotherapy
Input.Unnamed: 72: For metastatic NSCLC that has progressed on PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy, the current standard of care is typically docetaxel-based chemotherapy (with or without ramucirumab), pemetrexed (for non-squamous), or other platinum-based chemotherapy regimens. Clinical trial enrollment is also commonly considered at this stage.
Input.Unnamed: 73: For EGFR-mutated advanced NSCLC, current standard of care is first-line EGFR-TKI monotherapy, with osimertinib being the preferred third-generation TKI. FLAURA2 results support osimertinib plus chemotherapy as an emerging new standard.
Input.Unnamed: 74: For adjuvant treatment of ALK-positive NSCLC post-surgery, the current standard of care is adjuvant ALK inhibitor therapy (alectinib preferred based on ALINA trial results) following complete surgical resection. Adjuvant chemotherapy may also be considered. Observation was previously standard but is being replaced by targeted therapy.
Input.Unnamed: 75: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is the standard of care for most patients with clinically node-positive disease and for a large proportion of triple-negative and HER2-positive breast cancers. For high-risk, early-stage ER+/HER2- breast cancer, neoadjuvant chemotherapy is also standard, with pembrolizumab increasingly added for improved outcomes.
Input.Unnamed: 76: Surgery (breast-conserving surgery or mastectomy) is the standard initial treatment for DCIS. For most patients, this is followed by radiation therapy and/or hormone therapy (tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors for hormone receptor-positive cases). For low-risk DCIS, active surveillance is emerging as an alternative, but surgery remains the standard.

Input.Unnamed: 24: No direct competing trials for this biomarker-selected population
Input.Unnamed: 28: ADT intensification with abiraterone or other agents
Input.Unnamed: 30: Other AR inhibitor trials in biochemical recurrence
Input.Unnamed: 33: DESTINY-Breast06 (trastuzumab deruxtecan in HER2-low first-line), KATHERINE (trastuzumab emtansine adjuvant), other HER2-targeted ADCs in development for HER2-low disease
Input.Unnamed: 34: Alpelisib (SOLAR-1, NCT02437318), Capivasertib (CAPItello-291, NCT04862663), Elacestrant (EMERALD, NCT03778931), Other PI3K inhibitor trials in PIK3CA-mutated HR+/HER2- breast cancer
Input.Unnamed: 35: Other CD20×CD3 bispecifics (glofitamab, mosunetuzumab, odronextamab) in various FL trials, CAR-T therapies being evaluated in FL (axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel), tazemetostat for EZH2-mutant FL, PI3K inhibitors, and various combination immunotherapy trials in R/R FL
Input.Unnamed: 36: 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera - approved), everolimus, sunitinib. Competing trials: NETTER-2 (177Lu-DOTATATE in advanced GEP-NETs), COMPOSE trial (177Lu-edotreotide in aggressive Grade 2/3 GEP-NETs)

Input.Unnamed: 70: The EmpowHER-303 trial (NCT06435429) is currently enrolling participants as of November 2025. The study is a global, Phase 3, open-label trial evaluating zanidatamab in combination with physician's choice chemotherapy versus trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer who have progressed on or are intolerant to prior trastuzumab deruxtecan treatment. The trial is being conducted at approximately 170 sites worldwide and aims to enroll about 550 participants.

Input.Unnamed: 75: Phase 1 near completion as of November 2024, with 26 patients enrolled across three cohorts. Vaccine demonstrated safety and tolerability with injection site irritation as primary adverse event. Over 70% of patients exhibited protocol-defined immune responses. Final Phase 1 results to be presented at San Antonio Breast Cancer Symposium December 11, 2025. Phase 2 trial in neoadjuvant setting planned to commence in 2025, with IND sponsorship transferring from Cleveland Clinic to Anixa Biosciences.

Input.Unnamed: 46: Significant improvement in PFS (HR 0.53, 95% CI 0.41-0.68, p<0.001) and interim OS (HR 0.47, 95% CI 0.32-0.69); ORR 60.9% vs 40.0% (p=0.0008)

Input.Unnamed: 50: Significant improvement in disease-free survival (HR 0.50; 3-year DFS 86.4% vs 76.6%; p<0.0001)

Input.Unnamed: 58: Phase 1 results presented at SITC 2024: vaccine demonstrated safety and tolerability across all three cohorts; strong immune responses observed in Cohort 1a; similar safety profile in Cohort 1b with ongoing analysis; no major adverse events in Cohort 1c (vaccine + pembrolizumab) with confirmed immune responses

Input.Unnamed: 69: None reported for Phase 3 trial; Phase 2 data (NCT05880706) showed 100% ORR, 95% confirmed ORR, 92.1% 12-month PFS rate

Input.Unnamed: 74: Final analysis results disclosed (trial terminated April 2025): Adjuvant crizotinib did not improve DFS (HR not significantly different from 1.0) or OS compared to observation. Grade 3+ treatment-related adverse events occurred in 43% of crizotinib patients. Treatment discontinuation due to adverse effects occurred in 27% of patients.
Input.Unnamed: 75: Safe and well-tolerated across all three cohorts. Primary adverse event was injection site irritation; no flu-like symptoms, abnormal laboratory tests, or major systemic adverse events observed, including in combination with pembrolizumab. Over 70% of patients exhibited protocol-defined immune responses with α-lactalbumin-specific T cell induced interferon gamma and interleukin-17. Maximum tolerated dose successfully identified.
Input.Unnamed: 76: 2-year primary analysis results published December 2024: 2-year ipsilateral invasive cancer rate was 5.9% (95% CI, 3.71-8.04) in guideline-concordant care arm vs 4.2% (95% CI, 2.31-6.00) in active monitoring arm (difference 1.7%), demonstrating noninferiority. No significant differences in invasive tumor size, node status, or tumor grade between groups. Quality of life remained stable and similar between arms.

Input.Unnamed: 3: SERENA-2 showed PFS benefit vs fulvestrant
Input.Unnamed: 6: Strong preclinical synergy; ASCENT 2L validated SG
Input.Unnamed: 9: ARC-7/10 showed strong PFS/OS signal in PD-L1-high
Input.Unnamed: 11: High recurrence rates after surgery justify ICI trials
Input.Unnamed: 13: Phase 2: Promising efficacy in R/R NPM1-mutant AML; company has $609.7M cash to support through pivotal results
Input.Unnamed: 14: Phase 1/2: 59.3% ORR in progressive disease, 59.0% in treatment-naive patients; manageable safety
Input.Unnamed: 15: Previous ATR trials in NSCLC unsuccessful; limited clinical data
Input.Unnamed: 16: Shows activity in PD-L1-low or "cold" tumors; concern about CTLA-4 toxicity
Input.Unnamed: 17: Phase 2 (S1800A): 136 patients showed extended survival vs standard treatment
Input.Unnamed: 18: Demonstrated significant PFS and OS improvement; marks practice-changing data in HER2-low space
Input.Unnamed: 19: Antitumor activity in HER2+ and HER2-low models; manageable safety; expanded therapeutic window
Input.Unnamed: 20: First treatment to show OS improvement with PIK3CA pathway targeting; MSK led Phase 1 trial
Input.Unnamed: 21: More potent than T-DM1 in preclinical HER2+ and HER2-low models; no activity in cardiomyocytes
Input.Unnamed: 22: Prior approval for 2nd-line BRAF V600E mCRC; historically limited 1st-line efficacy for this mutation
Input.Unnamed: 23: ICIs beneficial in metastatic dMMR CRC; unclear if benefit extends to adjuvant setting
Input.Unnamed: 24: Rationale based on PI3K pathway role in CRC; aspirin well-established cardiovascular drug
Input.Unnamed: 25: Phase 2: 67% ORR with lifileucel + pembrolizumab; durable responses in ~20 patients treated
Input.Unnamed: 27: Phase 1/2: 25.5-month median PFS with combination; no added systemic toxicity vs anti-PD-1 monotherapy
Input.Unnamed: 28: MAGNITUDE trial (mCRPC): BRCA+ patients showed 47% rPFS risk reduction with Akeega
Input.Unnamed: 29: 3 intraprostatic injections with favorable tolerability; targets unmet need in localized disease
Input.Unnamed: 30: ARAMIS trial (nmCRPC): Darolutamide significantly improved mFS and OS
Input.Unnamed: 31: Established efficacy leading to FDA approval for maintenance indication
Input.Unnamed: 33: Phase 1/2 studies showed promising efficacy in HER2-low metastatic breast cancer with ORR of 37.0-37.5% and median PFS of 6.3-11.1 months in heavily pretreated patients, supporting Phase 3 development
Input.Unnamed: 34: Phase 1/2 showed significant improvement in PFS
Input.Unnamed: 35: Phase 1/2 showed 82% ORR with 60% CR in heavily pretreated R/R FL, with manageable safety profile and primarily low-grade CRS and ICANS events
Input.Unnamed: 36: Positive Phase 2 retrospective study results supported Phase 3 initiation and led to FDA Fast Track Designation
Input.Unnamed: 37: Phase 1b/2 EV-103 trial showed high response rates (ORR 73.3%, CR 15.6%) and manageable safety with enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab in first-line metastatic urothelial carcinoma; Phase 3 EV-302 trial demonstrated significant survival benefit (HR 0.47 for OS) in first-line metastatic disease, supporting investigation in earlier-stage MIBC
Input.Unnamed: 38: Phase 1 (DeLLphi-300): ORR 23.4%, median DOR 12.3 months, median OS 13.2 months in heavily pretreated SCLC; manageable safety profile with 31% grade ≥3 TRAEs. Phase 2 (DeLLphi-301): ORR 40% (10 mg dose) with durable responses; led to FDA accelerated approval May 2024.
Input.Unnamed: 39: Phase 1b/2 EMBER study: Imlunestrant demonstrated manageable safety profile and preliminary antitumor activity in ER+/HER2- advanced breast cancer, with median PFS of 7.2 months at 400 mg dose in heavily pretreated patients (92% prior CDK4/6i exposure). Recommended phase 2 dose established at 400 mg once daily.
Input.Unnamed: 40: Phase 2 coopERA trial (neoadjuvant setting) showed giredestrant was superior to aromatase inhibitor in reducing malignant cell division (Ki67 levels) in ER+ HER2- early breast cancer, supporting advancement to Phase 3 adjuvant trial
Input.Unnamed: 41: Phase 1/2 studies demonstrated manageable safety and promising efficacy, supporting the initiation of the phase 3 CABINET trial. Early data showed significant delays in disease progression and favorable safety profile, leading to the decision to advance to phase 3.
Input.Unnamed: 42: Phase 1b/2 results demonstrated a manageable safety profile and promising efficacy, with median progression-free survival (PFS) of 15.0 months for inavolisib + palbociclib + fulvestrant versus 7.3 months for placebo + palbociclib + fulvestrant, more than doubling PFS. Grade 3/4 hyperglycemia, stomatitis, diarrhea, and ocular toxic effects were reported, but no new safety signals or protocol modifications were disclosed.
Input.Unnamed: 43: Phase 1/2 LINKER-MM1 showed 71% ORR at 200mg dose with manageable safety profile; 59% achieved VGPR or better, 30% achieved CR or better; CRS mostly Grade 1 (35%), with 9.4% Grade 2 and single Grade 3 event
Input.Unnamed: 44: Phase 1/2 showed 73% ORR in 317 patients with CLL/SLL
Input.Unnamed: 45: Phase 1/2 data demonstrated acceptable safety profile and preliminary efficacy signals supporting advancement to Phase 3; specific Phase 2 results not publicly detailed in available sources
Input.Unnamed: 46: BEACON CRC trial demonstrated significant benefit in later-line setting, with improved response rates and survival in patients with BRAF V600E-mutated metastatic colorectal cancer.
Input.Unnamed: 47: Phase 1/2 trials demonstrated a median overall survival of 21.7 months in nonrecurrent H3K27M-mutant diffuse midline glioma patients, with a favorable safety profile and evidence of durable responses.
Input.Unnamed: 48: Phase 2 ELM-2 showed 52% ORR and 31.5% CR in heavily pretreated R/R DLBCL
Input.Unnamed: 49: Phase 2 studies demonstrated PARP inhibitor efficacy in BRCA-mutant cancers with manageable safety profile; olaparib showed activity in metastatic BRCA-mutant breast cancer (OlympiAD trial), supporting evaluation in adjuvant setting
Input.Unnamed: 50: Phase 2 data supported efficacy in dMMR/MSI-H colorectal cancer; immune checkpoint inhibitors showed benefit in metastatic dMMR setting, supporting adjuvant evaluation
Input.Unnamed: 51: CheckMate-142 (Phase 2) demonstrated durable responses and manageable safety with nivolumab + ipilimumab in previously treated MSI-H/dMMR mCRC, with ORR of 55% and 12-month PFS of 71%, supporting initiation of CheckMate-8HW for first-line evaluation
Input.Unnamed: 52: Phase 1 data showed 57% ORR in treatment-naive patients and 46% ORR in those previously treated with anti-PD-1, with 25-31% complete response rates and an acceptable safety profile.
Input.Unnamed: 53: Phase 2 showed significant improvement in pathologic complete response
Input.Unnamed: 54: Phase 2 KEYNOTE-016 showed durable responses with pembrolizumab in previously treated MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors including CRC, supporting Phase 3 development in first-line setting
Input.Unnamed: 55: Phase 2 waveLINE-007 (NCT05406401) showed 100% complete response rate at recommended Phase 2 dose of 1.75 mg/kg zilovertamab vedotin plus R-CHP in previously untreated DLBCL (presented ASH 2024)
Input.Unnamed: 56: Phase 1b CHRYSALIS study: Amivantamab + lazertinib demonstrated antitumor activity in treatment-naive EGFR+ NSCLC with manageable safety profile, supporting Phase 3 advancement
Input.Unnamed: 57: ABACUS (Phase 2) showed benefit of neoadjuvant durvalumab monotherapy in MIBC, with manageable safety profile, supporting Phase 3 NIAGARA trial design combining durvalumab with chemotherapy
Input.Unnamed: 58: Preclinical studies in mice showed safe and effective prevention of breast tumors through immune system activation against α-lactalbumin; provided rationale for Phase 1 human trial
Input.Unnamed: 59: Phase 1/2 (NCT05471843) met primary endpoint of ORR in heavily pretreated R/R MCL patients post-BTK inhibitor and anti-CD20 therapy, showing high response rates including complete responses, with manageable safety profile and no dose-limiting toxicities; results supported FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation in October 2025
Input.Unnamed: 60: Phase 1 data showed 55% three-year overall survival (vs 16% historical SOC), 38% disease-free at 3 years (vs 6% historical), median OS 38.4 months (vs 14.6 months historical), median PFS 17 months (vs 6.9 months historical), and 12-month disease-free survival 75% (vs 26.9% historical). No serious adverse events were reported, with minor side effects limited to fatigue, skin rash, and pruritis. Phase 2 randomized trial (124 patients) showed PFS improvement of 2.2 months (p=0.011) though OS primary endpoint was not met; vaccine was well tolerated with maintained quality of life.
Input.Unnamed: 61: Phase 2: ORR 45%, median PFS 8.2 months in pretreated NSCLC (published Lancet Oncology 2024); Phase 1b/2: Manageable safety profile, ORR 38% in SCLC (ASCO 2023); Phase 2: Superior PFS vs. chemotherapy (HR 0.55) in biomarker-selected population
Input.Unnamed: 62: Phase 2 metastatic breast cancer (OlympiAD): Olaparib showed significant PFS benefit vs chemotherapy in BRCA-mutated metastatic breast cancer (median PFS 7.0 vs 4.2 months, HR 0.58), with manageable safety profile, supporting Phase 3 adjuvant study
Input.Unnamed: 63: Phase 1 results (published April 2025): ORR 73.5% (25/34) in G12Ci-naive patients overall; 66.7% in NSCLC (14/21), 88.9% in CRC (8/9), 75.0% in PDAC (3/4). Favorable safety profile with no grade 4/5 treatment-related adverse events. Grade 3 TRAEs in 16.7% of patients. D3S-001 600 mg selected for Phase 2 expansion based on pharmacokinetics and efficacy.
Input.Unnamed: 64: Phase 1/2 data (OptiTROP-Lung01, NCT05351788) in EGFR-mutated NSCLC showed ORR of 48.6-77.6%, DCR of 94.6-100%, and median PFS of 15.4 months with manageable safety profile; Phase 2 data (OptiTROP-Lung03, NCT05631262) showed ORR of 45.1% vs 15.6% with docetaxel, supporting Phase 3 advancement
Input.Unnamed: 65: Phase 2 (NCT04165772): 100% clinical complete response (cCR) in all 42 patients who completed 6 months of dostarlimab treatment; sustained cCR with median follow-up of 26.3 months in first 24 patients; no grade 3 or higher adverse events reported; results published in NEJM 2022 and led to FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation
Input.Unnamed: 66: Phase 2 IDeate-Lung01 (NCT05280470): ORR 48.2% (95% CI 39.6-56.9%) at 12 mg/kg dose, median DOR 5.3 months, DCR 87.6%, median TTR 1.4 months (presented at WCLC September 2025). Phase 1/2 IDeate-PanTumor01 (NCT04145622): ORR 52.4%, median DOR 5.9 months, median OS 12.2 months in 21 ES-SCLC patients. Manageable safety profile supported Phase 3 advancement.
Input.Unnamed: 67: Phase 2 (TROPiCS-03): ORR 41.9% (95% CI, 27.0%-57.9%), median PFS 4.4 months, median OS 13.6 months in 2nd-line ES-SCLC; manageable safety profile consistent with prior studies (IASLC 2024)
Input.Unnamed: 68: Phase I/II SOHO-01 study demonstrated promising safety and efficacy as a second-line therapy in patients with advanced NSCLC and HER2 mutations, supporting progression to Phase III
Input.Unnamed: 69: Phase 2 combination data (iza-bren + osimertinib): 100% ORR, 95% confirmed ORR, 92.1% 12-month PFS rate, manageable hematologic toxicities (NCT05880706, presented WCLC 2025)
Input.Unnamed: 70: Phase 2: In the DESTINY-Breast03 trial, trastuzumab deruxtecan demonstrated a median progression-free survival (PFS) of 25.1 months versus 5.4 months with trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1), with an objective response rate (ORR) of 79.7% versus 34.2%. The safety profile was manageable, with the most common grade 3 or higher adverse events being neutropenia (12%) and interstitial lung disease (ILD) (10%). These results led to the initiation of Phase 3 trials for further evaluation in earlier lines of therapy.
Input.Unnamed: 71: Phase 2 HUDSON study: ORR 13.9%, median PFS 6.0 months, median OS 15.9 months in immunotherapy-refractory NSCLC; manageable safety profile supported Phase 3 advancement
Input.Unnamed: 72: Phase 1/2 results showed some activity in low TPS and STK-11/KEAP-1 mutated tumors, but with toxicity concerns
Input.Unnamed: 73: Phase 1/2 data for osimertinib plus chemotherapy combination not specifically detailed in available sources. FLAURA2 was designed based on rationale that combination therapy might overcome resistance mechanisms and improve outcomes beyond osimertinib monotherapy.
Input.Unnamed: 74: Phase 1/2 results demonstrated high response rates (approximately 60% ORR) and manageable safety profile in ALK-positive NSCLC, with progression-free survival of 7-10 months in metastatic setting, supporting progression to Phase 3 adjuvant trial.
Input.Unnamed: 75: Preclinical research by Dr. Vincent Tuohy at Cleveland Clinic showed that activating the immune system against α-lactalbumin was safe and effective in preventing breast tumors in mice. The vaccine targets a lactation protein that is re-expressed in many breast cancers, providing rationale for immunization as a preventive and therapeutic approach.
Input.Unnamed: 76: N/A - Phase 3 trial designed based on clinical rationale and observational data about DCIS overtreatment, not based on previous phase drug development results

Original.Unnamed: 38: Adults with histologically or cytologically confirmed extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) who experienced disease progression during or after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy with or without an immune checkpoint inhibitor. ECOG performance status 0 or 1 required. Patients with asymptomatic, previously treated or untreated brain metastases were eligible.

Original.Unnamed: 40: Patients with medium- or high-risk stage I to III ER-positive, HER2-negative early breast cancer who have completed definitive locoregional therapy (surgery with or without radiotherapy) and are candidates for adjuvant endocrine therapy
Original.Unnamed: 41: Adults with histologically-confirmed, well- or moderately-differentiated, locally advanced/unresectable or metastatic neuroendocrine tumors (pancreatic or non-pancreatic) who have documented disease progression within the 12 months prior to registration, and who have received prior systemic therapy.
Original.Unnamed: 42: Adults with hormone receptor-positive (HR+), HER2-negative (HER2-), locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer (MBC), with documented PIK3CA mutations, who have not received prior treatment with a PI3K inhibitor and have progressed after a CDK4/6 inhibitor plus an aromatase inhibitor.
Original.Unnamed: 43: Patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma who have received 1-4 prior lines of therapy, including lenalidomide and a proteasome inhibitor, and whose disease has progressed on or after the last therapy; prior anti-CD38 antibody treatment is permitted
Original.Unnamed: 44: Adults with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) or small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL) who are either treatment-naïve or have received prior therapy but have not been exposed to a Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor
Original.Unnamed: 45: Adults with ER-positive, HER2-negative, locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer who have progressed after prior treatment with CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy (in either the metastatic or adjuvant setting)
Original.Unnamed: 46: Adults with histologically or cytologically confirmed metastatic colorectal cancer harboring a BRAF V600E mutation, with measurable disease per RECIST v1.1, ECOG performance status 0-1, and adequate organ and marrow function. Patients must not have received prior systemic therapy for metastatic disease.
Original.Unnamed: 47: Adult and pediatric patients with newly diagnosed H3K27M-mutant diffuse glioma, aged 2 years and older, with measurable disease per RANO-HGG, and a Karnofsky/Lansky performance score (KPS/LPS) of ≥60
Original.Unnamed: 48: Adults with previously untreated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) with intermediate- or high-risk features (IPI score ≥2)
Original.Unnamed: 49: Adults with high-risk HER2-negative breast cancer who have a germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, following completion of local treatment and neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy as indicated by standard of care
Original.Unnamed: 50: Patients with surgically resected stage III dMMR colon cancer
Original.Unnamed: 51: Immunotherapy-naive adults with unresectable or metastatic colorectal cancer across different lines of therapy with microsatellite instability-high or mismatch repair-deficient status per local testing, and ECOG performance status 0 or 1
Original.Unnamed: 52: Previously untreated patients with unresectable stage III or stage IV metastatic melanoma, with at least one measurable lesion per RECIST v1.1, and no prior systemic anticancer therapy for unresectable or metastatic melanoma. Patients may have received prior adjuvant or neoadjuvant therapy if relapse occurred more than 6 months after treatment discontinuation.
Original.Unnamed: 53: Patients with newly diagnosed, previously untreated high-risk early-stage triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), including those with clinical stage I-III disease, with eligibility often based on tumor size (T1c or greater) and/or lymph node involvement.
Original.Unnamed: 54: Adults with previously untreated MSI-H/dMMR metastatic colorectal cancer and ECOG performance status 0 or 1
Original.Unnamed: 55: Adults with previously untreated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL)
Original.Unnamed: 56: Adults with previously untreated, locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) harboring EGFR exon 19 deletions or L858R mutations
Original.Unnamed: 57: Patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer (cT2-T4aN0/1M0), eligible for cisplatin-based chemotherapy, and fit for radical cystectomy
Original.Unnamed: 58: Patients with high-risk early-stage triple negative breast cancer (Stage I-III) who have completed treatment within 36 months, or patients at genetic risk for TNBC planning prophylactic mastectomy, or patients receiving adjuvant pembrolizumab
Original.Unnamed: 59: Adults with relapsed/refractory mantle cell lymphoma who have received prior therapy with a Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor and an anti-CD20 antibody
Original.Unnamed: 60: Newly diagnosed glioblastoma patients who are HLA-A2 positive, have undergone surgical resection, and are candidates for standard chemoradiation with temozolomide. Patients must have glioblastoma tissue available for MGMT methylation status assessment prior to randomization.
Original.Unnamed: 61: Patients with early stage, high-risk estrogen receptor-positive (ER+) HER2-negative (HER2-) breast cancer, specifically those with anatomic stage group II or III, or a subset of stage IIA, who have undergone complete surgical resection with clear margins and have no evidence of distant metastases or recurrence after curative surgery. Eligible patients may be pre- or postmenopausal women or men.
Original.Unnamed: 62: Adults with high-risk, HER2-negative breast cancer who have germline BRCA1/2 mutations and have completed surgery and chemotherapy for early-stage disease.
Original.Unnamed: 63: Patients with KRAS G12C-mutated locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC who have received chemotherapy and immunotherapy but have not received a KRAS G12C inhibitor
Original.Unnamed: 64: Patients with advanced or metastatic nonsquamous NSCLC harboring EGFR mutations, whose disease progressed following a tyrosine kinase inhibitor and platinum-based chemotherapy
Original.Unnamed: 65: Patients with histologically confirmed Stage II to III (T3-T4, N0, or T any, N+) locally advanced rectal cancer with mismatch repair deficient (dMMR) or microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) status, and no prior treatment for rectal cancer
Original.Unnamed: 66: Adults with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) who have progressed on or after platinum-based chemotherapy, with only one prior platinum-based line as systemic therapy for ES-SCLC, and adequate organ function
Original.Unnamed: 67: Adult patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) whose disease has progressed on or following platinum-based chemotherapy, with measurable disease per RECIST 1.1 criteria and ECOG performance status of 0 or 1
Original.Unnamed: 68: Adults with locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with documented activating HER2 mutations, who have progressed after at least one prior systemic therapy for advanced disease or are ineligible for standard treatments. Patients must have an ECOG performance status of 0 or 1 and a minimum life expectancy of 12 weeks.
Original.Unnamed: 69: Patients with EGFR-mutated non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) eligible for first-line treatment
Original.Unnamed: 70: Adults with histologically-proven HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer, with adequate organ and marrow function, ECOG performance status ≤1, and no prior invasive breast cancer (except DCIS) or other malignancies within the past 3 years (except non-melanoma skin cancer or carcinoma in situ of the cervix). Patients must be affiliated with a social security system or equivalent and able to comply with study procedures.
Original.Unnamed: 71: Patients with advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) without actionable genomic alterations (EGFR/ALK wild-type) who have progressed on or after prior anti-PD-(L)1 therapy and platinum-based chemotherapy, eligible for second- or third-line therapy, with ECOG performance status of 0 or 1
Original.Unnamed: 72: Adults with metastatic squamous non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) who have progressed on prior PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy (enrollment restricted to squamous histology as of December 2024)
Original.Unnamed: 73: Adults with previously untreated, locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) harboring EGFR mutations
Original.Unnamed: 74: Patients with completely resected stage IB (tumors ≥4 cm), II, or IIIA non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with ALK rearrangement, who have not received previous systemic anticancer therapy.
Original.Unnamed: 75: Three cohorts: 1a) Patients who completed treatment for early-stage triple-negative breast cancer within the past 3 years, currently tumor-free but at high risk for recurrence; 1b) Cancer-free individuals at high risk for developing breast cancer (primarily BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2 mutations) who have elected preventive mastectomy; 1c) Patients with early-stage triple-negative breast cancer with residual disease after neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy and surgery, receiving pembrolizumab post-surgery
Original.Unnamed: 76: Female patients aged 40 years or older with newly diagnosed, screen-detected, grade 1 or 2, hormone receptor-positive (estrogen and/or progesterone receptor ≥10% staining), HER2-negative ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) without evidence of invasive or microinvasive disease. Patients with breast symptoms or mass on baseline imaging were excluded. Concurrence of 2 pathologists required to confirm eligibility.

Original.Unnamed: 15: Prior failure of anti-PD-L1 and platinum therapy

Original.Unnamed: 24: PIK3CA mutations (exon 9/20) or other PI3K pathway mutations
Original.Unnamed: 28: HRR gene mutations (BRCA1/2 and other HRR genes; ~25% of mHSPC)
Original.Unnamed: 30: PSMA PET/CT positivity; PSA doubling time <12 months; PSA ≥0.2 ng/mL (post-RP) or ≥2 ng/mL above nadir (post-RT)
Original.Unnamed: 36: SSTR-positive status (confirmed by SSTR-PET imaging)
Original.Unnamed: 38: Not specified

Original.Unnamed: 41: No specific biomarker requirements for eligibility; only histologic confirmation of well- or moderately-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor is required.
Original.Unnamed: 42: PIK3CA gene mutations, HR+, HER2-
Original.Unnamed: 44: No specific biomarker requirements
Original.Unnamed: 45: ER+, HER2-
Original.Unnamed: 47: H3K27M mutation confirmed by immunohistochemistry or next-generation sequencing
Original.Unnamed: 48: CD20+ (cluster of differentiation 20 positive)
Original.Unnamed: 49: Germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation
Original.Unnamed: 50: dMMR/MSI-H
Original.Unnamed: 51: MSI-H/dMMR
Original.Unnamed: 52: BRAF V600 mutation status assessment required for eligibility; patients with BRAFV600 mutation-positive melanoma are eligible if treatment naive and without rapidly progressive disease.
Original.Unnamed: 53: Triple negative (ER < 10%, PR < 10%, HER2-negative per ASCO/CAP guidelines); no specific PD-L1 or other biomarker requirement for eligibility in most neoadjuvant trials, though PD-L1 status may be assessed for exploratory purposes.
Original.Unnamed: 54: MSI-H/dMMR
Original.Unnamed: 58: Triple negative (ER <1%, PR <1%, HER2-negative per ASCO/CAP guidelines)
Original.Unnamed: 59: Histologically confirmed mantle cell lymphoma with cyclin D1 expression and/or t(11;14) translocation
Original.Unnamed: 60: HLA-A2+ status, MGMT methylation status assessment
Original.Unnamed: 61: ER+ (positive for ER and/or PgR), HER2-negative
Original.Unnamed: 62: Germline BRCA1/2 mutations, HER2-negative
Original.Unnamed: 63: KRAS G12C mutation
Original.Unnamed: 65: Mismatch repair deficient (dMMR) or microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H)
Original.Unnamed: 66: No specific biomarker requirements for eligibility; tumor tissue samples required for B7-H3 expression analysis
Original.Unnamed: 69: EGFR mutations
Original.Unnamed: 70: HER2-positive (defined as HER2 3+ immunohistochemical overexpression or HER2 amplification by ASCO-CAP guidelines)
Original.Unnamed: 71: EGFR and ALK wild-type (no actionable genomic alterations)
Original.Unnamed: 72: Not specified
Original.Unnamed: 73: EGFR mutations (including exon 19 deletions and exon 21 L858R)
Original.Unnamed: 74: ALK rearrangement (fusion) detected by an FDA-approved test
Original.Unnamed: 75: Triple-negative status (ER-negative, PR-negative, HER2-negative); Cohort 1b specifically requires BRCA1, BRCA2, or PALB2 mutations
Original.Unnamed: 76: Hormone receptor-positive (ER and/or PR ≥10% staining), HER2-negative

Original.Unnamed: 18: HER2-targeted antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) with topoisomerase I inhibitor payload
Original.Unnamed: 21: Site-specific, construct-homogeneous ADC (DAR ~2) using EuCODE technology
Original.Unnamed: 25: Autologous tumor infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy + anti-PD-1 antibody
Original.Unnamed: 27: Therapeutic cancer vaccine (T-win platform) targeting IDO1 and PD-L1 pathways + anti-PD-1
Original.Unnamed: 28: PARP inhibitor (niraparib) + androgen synthesis inhibitor (abiraterone) + corticosteroid
Original.Unnamed: 31: RNA polymerase II inhibitor (lurbinectedin) + anti-PD-L1 antibody (atezolizumab)
Original.Unnamed: 33: Antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting HER2 with a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload, designed to selectively deliver cytotoxic drug to tumor cells expressing HER2 antigen.
Original.Unnamed: 34: Selective PI3Kα inhibitor that blocks the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway and promotes degradation of mutated p110α protein
Original.Unnamed: 35: CD20×CD3 bispecific antibody that binds CD20 on B-cells and CD3 on T-cells, leading to T-cell activation and B-cell lysis
Original.Unnamed: 36: Targeted radiopharmaceutical therapy - radiolabeled somatostatin analog delivers beta radiation to somatostatin receptor-positive (SSTR+) tumors, inducing DNA damage and cell death through internalization of the ligand-receptor complex and subsequent ionizing radiation effects.
Original.Unnamed: 37: Nectin-4 antibody-drug conjugate (enfortumab vedotin) binds to Nectin-4 on tumor cells, is internalized, and releases the microtubule-disrupting agent MMAE, causing cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis; combined with a PD-1 inhibitor (pembrolizumab) which blocks PD-1 on T cells to enhance anti-tumor immune response
Original.Unnamed: 38: Bispecific T-cell engager targeting DLL3 and CD3, which binds DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, bringing them into close proximity to form an immunological synapse, resulting in T-cell activation, release of inflammatory cytokines (such as perforin and granzyme B), and lysis of DLL3-expressing tumor cells.
Original.Unnamed: 39: Oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that binds to the estrogen receptor, induces an inactive conformation, and promotes proteasome-mediated degradation of the receptor protein, thereby inhibiting estrogen-dependent and independent ER signaling.
Original.Unnamed: 40: Oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD)
Original.Unnamed: 41: Oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor that inhibits multiple receptor tyrosine kinases including VEGFR2, MET, AXL, and RET, thereby blocking downstream signaling pathways involved in tumor cell proliferation, angiogenesis, migration, invasion, and drug resistance.
Original.Unnamed: 42: Inavolisib is a selective PI3Kα inhibitor that blocks the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway by inhibiting the catalytic activity of the p110α subunit, thereby reducing PIP3 production and downstream signaling involved in cell growth, survival, and metabolism.
Original.Unnamed: 43: BCMA×CD3 bispecific antibody that bridges BCMA on myeloma cells with CD3 on T cells to induce T-cell activation and targeted lysis of myeloma cells
Original.Unnamed: 44: Non-covalent (reversible) BTK inhibitor that binds to Bruton's tyrosine kinase and inhibits its activity, blocking B-cell receptor signaling and downstream survival pathways in malignant B-cells
Original.Unnamed: 45: Oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that binds to and promotes degradation of the estrogen receptor, combined with an mTOR inhibitor that blocks the mTOR signaling pathway, thereby inhibiting cell proliferation and survival in cancer cells
Original.Unnamed: 46: Combined inhibition of BRAF and EGFR blocks the MAPK signaling pathway at multiple points, preventing adaptive feedback reactivation and maintaining suppression of cell proliferation signals in BRAF V600E-mutant colorectal cancer.
Original.Unnamed: 47: ONC201 is a dopamine receptor D2 (DRD2) antagonist and mitochondrial ClpP protease agonist. It induces the integrated stress response (ISR) and tumor necrosis factor-related apoptosis-inducing ligand (TRAIL) pathways, leading to upregulation of death receptor 5 (DR5), inhibition of the AKT/mTOR/S6 pathways, and activation of apoptosis and cell cycle arrest. Its cytotoxic effects are mediated through both DRD2 antagonism and ClpP activation, with ClpP activation being essential for its antitumor activity.
Original.Unnamed: 48: CD20×CD3 bispecific antibody that binds CD20 on B-cells and CD3 on T-cells, leading to T-cell activation and B-cell lysis
Original.Unnamed: 49: PARP inhibitor that blocks PARP catalytic activity and traps PARP on DNA, preventing DNA repair and causing synthetic lethality in BRCA-deficient cells
Original.Unnamed: 50: PD-L1 inhibitor (immune checkpoint inhibitor) that blocks PD-L1/PD-1 interaction to restore T cell-mediated anti-tumor immune responses
Original.Unnamed: 51: Dual immune checkpoint blockade: nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor) and ipilimumab (CTLA-4 inhibitor) work synergistically to release T cell inhibition and enhance antitumor immune responses
Original.Unnamed: 52: Dual checkpoint inhibition targeting both LAG-3 and PD-1: fianlimab blocks LAG-3, preventing its interaction with MHC class II and other ligands, while cemiplimab blocks PD-1, preventing its interaction with PD-L1 and PD-L2. This dual blockade reactivates exhausted T cells and enhances anti-tumor immune responses by overcoming complementary inhibitory pathways.
Original.Unnamed: 53: Pembrolizumab is a monoclonal antibody that binds to the PD-1 receptor on T cells, blocking its interaction with PD-L1 and PD-L2 ligands. This blockade prevents the inhibition of T cell activation and restores the immune response against tumor cells.
Original.Unnamed: 54: PD-1 inhibitor: blocks the interaction between PD-1 on T cells and PD-L1 on tumor cells, preventing immune suppression and restoring T cell-mediated anti-tumor activity
Original.Unnamed: 55: ROR1-targeting antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) that binds to ROR1 on tumor cells, is internalized, and releases a cytotoxic payload (MMAE) to induce tumor cell death
Original.Unnamed: 56: Amivantamab: Bispecific antibody targeting EGFR and MET with immune cell-directing activity, ligand blocking, and receptor degradation; Lazertinib: Third-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI)
Original.Unnamed: 57: PD-L1 inhibitor that blocks the interaction between PD-L1 on tumor cells and PD-1 on T cells, thereby preventing immune suppression and restoring T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity
Original.Unnamed: 58: Cancer vaccine that stimulates immune system to recognize alpha-lactalbumin antigen, leading to antigen presentation by dendritic cells and activation of T cells for anti-tumor immune response
Original.Unnamed: 59: BCL2 inhibitor that selectively binds to BCL-2, displacing pro-apoptotic proteins and triggering apoptosis in cancer cells
Original.Unnamed: 60: Dendritic cell vaccine with synthetic peptides targeting tumor-associated antigens, designed to activate dendritic cells and induce antigen-specific T-cell responses against tumor cells
Original.Unnamed: 61: Selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that binds to the estrogen receptor, induces a conformational change, and promotes its degradation via the ubiquitin-proteasome system, thereby inhibiting estrogen signaling and tumor growth.
Original.Unnamed: 62: PARP inhibitor that blocks DNA repair and traps PARP proteins on DNA, causing synthetic lethality in cells with homologous recombination deficiency (e.g., BRCA1/2 mutations)
Original.Unnamed: 63: KRAS G12C inhibitors covalently bind to the mutated cysteine residue at position 12 of KRAS, trapping the protein in an inactive GDP-bound state and preventing nucleotide exchange to the active GTP-bound form, thereby inhibiting downstream oncogenic signaling.
Original.Unnamed: 64: TROP2-targeted antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) with a novel pyrimidine-thiol linker conjugated to a belotecan-derivative topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DAR 7.4); the hydrolytically sensitive linker permits both extracellular pH-sensitive cleavage and intracellular enzymatic cleavage to release the membrane-permeable payload, enabling a bystander effect
Original.Unnamed: 65: PD-1 inhibitor (monoclonal antibody that blocks PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T cell-mediated anti-tumor immune responses)
Original.Unnamed: 66: B7-H3-directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) comprising a humanized anti-B7-H3 IgG1 monoclonal antibody attached to topoisomerase I inhibitor payloads (exatecan derivative, DXd) via tetrapeptide-based cleavable linkers, designed to enhance selective tumor cell death and reduce systemic exposure
Original.Unnamed: 67: Antibody-drug conjugate targeting TROP2 with topoisomerase I inhibitor payload
Original.Unnamed: 68: Oral, reversible tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) that potently inhibits mutant HER2 (including HER2 exon 20 insertions and HER2 point mutations) and EGFR, with high selectivity for mutant vs wild-type EGFR
Original.Unnamed: 69: Bispecific antibody-drug conjugate targeting EGFR and HER3, binding to both receptors on tumor cells, internalizing via endocytosis, and releasing a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload to induce tumor cell death.
Original.Unnamed: 70: HER2-directed antibody-drug conjugate consisting of trastuzumab linked to a topoisomerase I inhibitor (deruxtecan) via a stable, tumor-selective cleavable linker. The antibody binds HER2 on tumor cells, leading to internalization, lysosomal degradation, and release of the cytotoxic payload, which induces DNA damage and cell death. The drug also exhibits immune-boosting effects such as antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC).
Original.Unnamed: 71: ATR kinase inhibitor (ceralasertib) disrupts DNA damage response and activates innate immune pathways (cGAS-STING), combined with PD-L1 inhibitor (durvalumab) to restore T cell function and enhance antitumor immunity
Original.Unnamed: 72: Anti-CTLA-4 monoclonal antibody that blocks the inhibitory signal resulting from CTLA-4 binding to CD80/86, leading to prolongation and enhancement of T-cell activation and expansion. This results in increased activation and expansion of tumor antigen-specific cytotoxic T cells (CTLs) and may reduce the suppressive activity of regulatory T cells (Tregs) in the tumor microenvironment.
Original.Unnamed: 73: EGFR-TKI inhibits EGFR signaling pathways, blocking tumor cell proliferation and survival, while chemotherapy (platinum-pemetrexed) induces DNA damage and cell death in rapidly dividing cancer cells. The combination enhances anti-tumor efficacy through synergistic effects, potentially overcoming or delaying resistance.
Original.Unnamed: 74: Crizotinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that binds to the ATP-binding pocket of the abnormal ALK protein, blocking its kinase activity and thereby inhibiting downstream signaling pathways involved in cell proliferation and survival.
Original.Unnamed: 75: Therapeutic cancer vaccine targeting α-lactalbumin, a lactation-associated protein typically expressed only in breast tissue during lactation but re-expressed in many forms of breast cancer including TNBC. The vaccine activates the immune system to direct cytotoxic T cells against tumor cells expressing α-lactalbumin, providing preemptive immune protection against emerging breast tumors. Induces α-lactalbumin-specific T cell responses with interferon gamma and interleukin-17 production.
Original.Unnamed: 76: N/A - comparing management strategies (active monitoring vs. surgery), not drug mechanisms

Original.Unnamed: 35: Glofitamab (approved CD20×CD3 bispecific), mosunetuzumab (approved CD20×CD3 bispecific), odronextamab (CD20×CD3 bispecific in development), rituximab (approved anti-CD20 antibody), obinutuzumab (approved anti-CD20 antibody), tazemetostat (approved EZH2 inhibitor for FL), lenalidomide (approved immunomodulatory drug), and various PI3K inhibitors
