Original.Unnamed: 38: Lurbinectedin (approved 2nd-line SCLC), topotecan (approved 2nd-line SCLC), atezolizumab and durvalumab (approved 1st-line with chemotherapy), ifinatamab deruxtecan (DLL3-targeting ADC in development), rovalpituzumab tesirine (DLL3-targeting ADC, discontinued)
Original.Unnamed: 40: Fulvestrant (approved SERD), Elacestrant (approved oral SERD), Camizestrant, Imlunestrant, Lasofoxifene, Amcenestrant (investigational oral SERDs)
Original.Unnamed: 41: Cabozantinib (CABOMETYX) is the approved drug in this class for advanced neuroendocrine tumors. Other tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) approved for neuroendocrine tumors include sunitinib (Sutent) and everolimus (Afinitor). Notable investigational agents in development for neuroendocrine tumors include surufatinib (a multi-targeted TKI), lenvatinib (Lenvima), and various peptide receptor radionuclide therapies (PRRT) such as lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (Lutathera).
Original.Unnamed: 42: Palbociclib, Ribociclib, Abemaciclib, Fulvestrant, Letrozole, Anastrozole, Exemestane, Tamoxifen, Imlunestrant, Elacestrant, Giredestrant, Camizestrant, Amcenestrant, AZD9833, AZD9496, Onapristone, ZN-c3, Inavolisib (PI3K inhibitor), Capivasertib (AKT inhibitor), Alpelisib (PI3K inhibitor), Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor)
Original.Unnamed: 43: Approved BCMA-targeted therapies: Teclistamab (Tecvayli), elranatamab (Elrexfio); CAR-T therapies: cilta-cel (Carvykti), ide-cel (Abecma); Other approved drugs for RRMM: daratumumab, isatuximab, elotuzumab, bortezomib, carfilzomib, lenalidomide, pomalidomide. In-trial: Other BCMA bispecific antibodies (talquetamab, alnuctamab, ABBV-383)
Original.Unnamed: 44: Ibrutinib (Imbruvica, approved covalent BTK inhibitor and comparator in this trial), acalabrutinib (Calquence, approved covalent BTK inhibitor), zanubrutinib (Brukinsa, approved covalent BTK inhibitor), venetoclax (Venclexta, approved BCL-2 inhibitor)
Original.Unnamed: 45: Fulvestrant (approved SERD), Elacestrant (approved oral SERD), Camizestrant (investigational SERD), Imlunestrant (investigational SERD)
Original.Unnamed: 46: Bevacizumab, cetuximab, panitumumab, ziv-aflibercept, ramucirumab, and investigational agents such as tucatinib + trastuzumab (MOUNTAINEER-03 trial), FOLFOXIRI-bevacizumab, and other EGFR and VEGF inhibitors in development.
Original.Unnamed: 47: Daratumumab (approved anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody), isatuximab (approved anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody), carfilzomib (approved proteasome inhibitor), lenalidomide (approved immunomodulatory drug), bortezomib (approved proteasome inhibitor), pomalidomide (approved immunomodulatory drug), and investigational agents such as bispecific T cell engagers and on-body delivery systems for monoclonal antibodies.
Original.Unnamed: 48: Rituximab (approved CD20 monoclonal antibody), polatuzumab vedotin (approved CD79b ADC for DLBCL), glofitamab (approved CD20xCD3 bispecific), epcoritamab (approved CD20xCD3 bispecific), mosunetuzumab (approved CD20xCD3 bispecific), axicabtagene ciloleucel (approved CAR-T for DLBCL), tisagenlecleucel (approved CAR-T for DLBCL), lisocabtagene maraleucel (approved CAR-T for DLBCL)
Original.Unnamed: 49: Talazoparib (approved for BRCA-mutant metastatic breast cancer), niraparib, rucaparib (PARP inhibitors in development for breast cancer)
Original.Unnamed: 50: Pembrolizumab and nivolumab (approved PD-1 inhibitors for metastatic dMMR/MSI-H colorectal cancer); durvalumab (PD-L1 inhibitor in development); standard chemotherapy regimens (FOLFOX, CAPOX) used in adjuvant setting
Original.Unnamed: 51: Pembrolizumab (approved for MSI-H/dMMR mCRC, KEYNOTE-177); dostarlimab (approved for dMMR solid tumors); other PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in development for MSI-H/dMMR cancers
Original.Unnamed: 52: Pembrolizumab, nivolumab, relatlimab (Opdualag), ipilimumab, cemiplimab, and other PD-1/LAG-3 or PD-1/CTLA-4 checkpoint inhibitor combinations are approved or in development for advanced melanoma. Notable investigational agents include tebentafusp (TCR bispecific), lifileucel (TIL therapy), and other bispecific antibodies targeting LAG-3, PD-1, or CTLA-4.
Original.Unnamed: 53: Atezolizumab (PD-L1 inhibitor), nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor), durvalumab (PD-L1 inhibitor), sacituzumab govitecan (TROP2-targeting ADC)
Original.Unnamed: 54: Nivolumab (approved PD-1 inhibitor for MSI-H/dMMR CRC), nivolumab + ipilimumab combination, dostarlimab (PD-1 inhibitor in development)
Original.Unnamed: 55: Polatuzumab vedotin (approved CD79b-targeting ADC for DLBCL), loncastuximab tesirine (approved CD19-targeting ADC), rituximab (approved anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody), other investigational ROR1-targeting ADCs and bispecific antibodies in development for B-cell lymphomas
Original.Unnamed: 56: Osimertinib (approved 3rd-generation EGFR TKI), gefitinib, erlotinib, afatinib (approved 1st/2nd-generation EGFR TKIs), mobocertinib (approved for EGFR exon 20 insertions), other EGFR-MET bispecific antibodies in development
Original.Unnamed: 57: Pembrolizumab (approved PD-1 inhibitor for bladder cancer), nivolumab (approved PD-1 inhibitor), atezolizumab (approved PD-L1 inhibitor), enfortumab vedotin (approved antibody-drug conjugate), avelumab (approved PD-L1 inhibitor for maintenance therapy)
Original.Unnamed: 58: Pembrolizumab (approved PD-1 inhibitor for TNBC in combination with chemotherapy); other cancer vaccines in development for breast cancer prevention
Original.Unnamed: 59: Venetoclax (approved BCL2 inhibitor for CLL/SLL), BTK inhibitors (zanubrutinib, acalabrutinib, pirtobrutinib), brexucabtagene autoleucel (approved CAR-T for R/R MCL)
Original.Unnamed: 60: Temozolomide (approved), lomustine (approved), bevacizumab (approved for recurrent GBM), dose-dense temozolomide regimens (investigational), and various dendritic cell vaccines and immunotherapies in clinical development (e.g., ICT-107, DCVax-L).
Original.Unnamed: 61: Abemaciclib (Verzenio), Palbociclib (Ibrance), Ribociclib (Kisqali); investigational agents include trilaciclib, lerociclib, and novel SERDs in combination with CDK4/6 inhibitors
Original.Unnamed: 62: Talazoparib (approved PARP inhibitor for metastatic BRCA+ breast cancer), niraparib and rucaparib (PARP inhibitors approved for ovarian cancer)
Original.Unnamed: 63: Approved KRAS G12C inhibitors include sotorasib (Lumakras) and adagrasib (Krazati). Other investigational KRAS G12C inhibitors in development include GDC-6036 (divarasib), JDQ443, and LY3537982.
Original.Unnamed: 64: Sacituzumab govitecan (approved TROP2 ADC for TNBC), datopotamab deruxtecan (TROP2 ADC in Phase 3 for NSCLC), osimertinib (approved third-generation EGFR TKI), amivantamab (approved EGFR-MET bispecific antibody for EGFR exon20 insertion), mobocertinib (approved EGFR exon20 insertion inhibitor), lazertinib (third-generation EGFR TKI in development)
Original.Unnamed: 65: Pembrolizumab (approved PD-1 inhibitor for dMMR/MSI-H cancers), nivolumab (approved PD-1 inhibitor), other investigational PD-1 inhibitors in development for dMMR/MSI-H cancers
Original.Unnamed: 66: Tarlatamab (approved DLL3 bispecific for ES-SCLC), lurbinectedin (approved for relapsed SCLC), topotecan (approved for relapsed SCLC), nivolumab (approved for SCLC maintenance), pembrolizumab (approved in combination for ES-SCLC), AMG 757 (DLL3 bispecific in development)
Original.Unnamed: 67: Lurbinectedin (approved for 2nd-line ES-SCLC), tarlatamab (DLL3 bispecific, approved 2024), topotecan (traditional 2nd-line option). Other TROP2-targeting ADCs in development include datopotamab deruxtecan. DLL3-targeting bispecifics in development include ifinatamab deruxtecan.
Original.Unnamed: 68: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu, approved HER2-targeting ADC for HER2-mutant NSCLC), poziotinib (HER2 TKI in development), and other investigational HER2-targeting agents in clinical trials
Original.Unnamed: 69: Osimertinib (approved 1st-line EGFR TKI), amivantamab (EGFR x MET bispecific antibody), patritumab deruxtecan (HER3 ADC in development), other EGFR x HER3 bispecific ADCs in development (AVZO-1418/DB-1418)
Original.Unnamed: 70: Trastuzumab, pertuzumab, trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1), trastuzumab rezetecan, zongertinib, tucatinib, and other HER2-targeted therapies (including monoclonal antibodies, antibody-drug conjugates, and kinase inhibitors) are approved or in development for HER2-positive breast cancer.
Original.Unnamed: 71: Docetaxel (approved 2nd-line NSCLC), other checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, atezolizumab), other ATR inhibitors in development (berzosertib, elimusertib)
Original.Unnamed: 72:
Approved: Ipilimumab (Yervoy, approved CTLA-4 inhibitor), docetaxel, pemetrexed, ramucirumab + docetaxel
In-trial: Other anti-CTLA-4 antibodies in development, novel immunotherapy combinations
Original.Unnamed: 73: Gefitinib, erlotinib, afatinib, dacomitinib (approved EGFR-TKIs); lazertinib, amivantamab (approved combinations); other EGFR-TKI combinations in development
Original.Unnamed: 74: Alectinib (approved ALK inhibitor, adjuvant setting), brigatinib (approved ALK inhibitor), lorlatinib (approved ALK inhibitor), ceritinib (approved ALK inhibitor), ensartinib (investigational ALK inhibitor)
Original.Unnamed: 75: Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) and nivolumab (Opdivo) - approved PD-1 inhibitors for TNBC in combination with chemotherapy; Other investigational cancer vaccines in development for breast cancer prevention and treatment
Original.Unnamed: 76: Tamoxifen, anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane (approved hormone therapies for hormone receptor-positive DCIS); ongoing trials (LORD, LORIS, LORETTA, RECAST) evaluating active surveillance with or without endocrine therapy

Original.Unnamed: 22: SOC: mFOLFOX6, FOLFOXIRI, or CAPOX ± bevacizumab
Original.Unnamed: 33: For HER2-low metastatic breast cancer after prior therapy, standard of care is physician's choice chemotherapy (capecitabine, eribulin, gemcitabine, or taxanes). For HER2-positive disease, first-line standard is taxane plus trastuzumab and pertuzumab.
Original.Unnamed: 34: CDK4/6 inhibitor combined with endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitor or fulvestrant) for first-line treatment; alpelisib plus fulvestrant for PIK3CA-mutated patients after progression on endocrine therapy
Original.Unnamed: 35: For relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma, standard of care includes rituximab plus lenalidomide (R2), bendamustine plus rituximab, CHOP or CVP-based chemoimmunotherapy, or other anti-CD20 antibody-based regimens. Treatment selection depends on prior therapies, response duration, and patient factors.
Original.Unnamed: 36: Somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) for symptom control and disease stabilization; 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) for eligible patients with progressive disease; everolimus or sunitinib for progressive disease
Original.Unnamed: 37: Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy + radical cystectomy (for cisplatin-eligible); immediate radical cystectomy (for cisplatin-ineligible)
Original.Unnamed: 38: Second-line chemotherapy options: topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin (investigator's choice)
Original.Unnamed: 39: For metastatic HR+ breast cancer, standard of care is endocrine therapy, typically aromatase inhibitors (AIs) or fulvestrant, often combined with CDK4/6 inhibitors in the first-line setting. For early-stage HR+ breast cancer, standard of care is 5-10 years of endocrine therapy (tamoxifen, AIs, or sequential therapy) after surgery.
Original.Unnamed: 40: Adjuvant endocrine therapy with aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane) for postmenopausal women or tamoxifen for premenopausal women, typically for 5-10 years
Original.Unnamed: 41: For previously treated, progressive advanced neuroendocrine tumors (pancreatic and extra-pancreatic), the standard of care is typically a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) such as sunitinib or everolimus for pancreatic NETs, or everolimus for extra-pancreatic NETs. For patients who have progressed on prior TKIs, options may include peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) with lutetium Lu 177 dotatate, or chemotherapy regimens such as capecitabine and temozolomide. Cabozantinib is now an approved option for second-line or later therapy.
Original.Unnamed: 42: CDK4/6 inhibitors combined with endocrine therapy (such as aromatase inhibitors or fulvestrant) is the standard first-line treatment for hormone receptor-positive (HR+), HER2-negative (HER2-) advanced or metastatic breast cancer. For early breast cancer, CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib, ribociclib) are approved in specific high-risk populations in combination with endocrine therapy.
Original.Unnamed: 43: For relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma after 1-4 prior lines, standard regimens include combinations of immunomodulatory drugs (lenalidomide, pomalidomide), proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, carfilzomib), anti-CD38 antibodies (daratumumab, isatuximab), and corticosteroids. Increasingly, bispecific antibodies (teclistamab, elranatamab) and CAR-T therapies (cilta-cel, ide-cel) are used in later lines.
Original.Unnamed: 44: BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) as monotherapy or venetoclax-based combinations for most patients; chemoimmunotherapy (bendamustine-rituximab, FCR) for select fit patients without high-risk features
Original.Unnamed: 45: CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitor or fulvestrant) in 1st-line; endocrine therapy (fulvestrant, aromatase inhibitor, or exemestane) plus everolimus in 2nd-line post-CDK4/6 inhibitor progression
Original.Unnamed: 46: For patients with metastatic colorectal cancer, the current standard of care is mFOLFOX6 or FOLFIRI chemotherapy combined with either bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) or cetuximab/panitumumab (anti-EGFR) for those with RAS wild-type tumors. For BRAF V600E-mutated tumors, encorafenib + cetuximab is now FDA-approved in the first-line setting.
Original.Unnamed: 47: For newly diagnosed multiple myeloma, the current standard of care is a four-drug combination regimen such as daratumumab plus carfilzomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone (DKRd), especially for transplant-eligible patients. For standard- and intermediate-risk patients, bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone (VRd) remains a standard triplet induction. Induction therapy is followed by autologous stem cell transplant (if eligible) and maintenance therapy, typically with lenalidomide.
Original.Unnamed: 48: R-CHOP (rituximab plus cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) for first-line treatment of DLBCL
Original.Unnamed: 49: Chemotherapy, surgery, and radiation therapy as indicated; olaparib is now approved as adjuvant therapy for this population based on OlympiA trial results
Original.Unnamed: 50: mFOLFOX6 alone (historical standard); atezolizumab + mFOLFOX6 (new standard of care based on ATOMIC trial results, ASCO 2025)
Original.Unnamed: 51: First-line: Pembrolizumab monotherapy or nivolumab + ipilimumab (both FDA-approved 2025); chemotherapy (FOLFOX/FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab/cetuximab) is alternative option
Original.Unnamed: 52: For previously untreated patients with advanced or metastatic melanoma, the current standard of care is combination immunotherapy with a PD-1 inhibitor (e.g., pembrolizumab or nivolumab) plus a CTLA-4 inhibitor (ipilimumab) or a PD-1/LAG-3 inhibitor combination (e.g., relatlimab + nivolumab, Opdualag). For BRAF V600 mutation-positive patients, targeted therapy with BRAF/MEK inhibitors is also an option.
Original.Unnamed: 53: Anthracycline and taxane-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy, often with platinum agents (carboplatin), for high-risk early-stage TNBC
Original.Unnamed: 54: FOLFOX or FOLFIRI chemotherapy ± bevacizumab or cetuximab (historical standard); pembrolizumab is now standard of care for MSI-H/dMMR mCRC
Original.Unnamed: 55: R-CHOP (rituximab plus cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) for most patients with previously untreated DLBCL
Original.Unnamed: 56: Osimertinib (third-generation EGFR TKI) as first-line treatment for EGFR-mutated (exon 19del or L858R) advanced NSCLC
Original.Unnamed: 57: Cisplatin-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (typically gemcitabine + cisplatin) followed by radical cystectomy
Original.Unnamed: 58: Adjuvant chemotherapy (anthracycline and taxane-based regimens) for high-risk TNBC; pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy in neoadjuvant setting for PD-L1 positive tumors
Original.Unnamed: 59: For relapsed/refractory mantle cell lymphoma after BTK inhibitor and anti-CD20 therapy, standard options include alternative BTK inhibitors (if not previously used), chemotherapy regimens (bendamustine, cytarabine-based), venetoclax-based combinations, and CAR-T cell therapy (brexucabtagene autoleucel) in eligible patients
Original.Unnamed: 60: Maximum safe surgical resection followed by concurrent radiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) and temozolomide (75 mg/m²/day for 6 weeks), then adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m²/day for 5 days every 28 days for up to 6 cycles).
Original.Unnamed: 61: CDK4/6 inhibitors combined with endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitors or fulvestrant) are the standard first-line treatment for hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative advanced or metastatic breast cancer, and are increasingly used in the adjuvant setting for high-risk early breast cancer.
Original.Unnamed: 62: Surgery and chemotherapy (neoadjuvant or adjuvant), with endocrine therapy for hormone receptor-positive disease
Original.Unnamed: 63: For patients with KRAS G12C-mutated locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC who have received prior chemotherapy and immunotherapy, KRAS G12C inhibitors such as sotorasib or adagrasib are approved treatment options. Other options may include docetaxel or other chemotherapy regimens.
Original.Unnamed: 64: For patients with advanced or metastatic nonsquamous NSCLC harboring EGFR mutations who have progressed following a tyrosine kinase inhibitor and platinum-based chemotherapy, the current standard of care includes chemotherapy (docetaxel or pemetrexed) or targeted therapy with amivantamab (for EGFR exon20 insertion mutations) or mobocertinib (for EGFR exon20 insertion mutations)
Original.Unnamed: 65: Traditional standard: neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by surgery. Emerging practice: neoadjuvant PD-1 inhibitor monotherapy (based on recent breakthrough data showing 100% clinical complete response rates in dMMR/MSI-H rectal cancer)
Original.Unnamed: 66: For patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer who have progressed after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, standard options include topotecan, lurbinectedin, or tarlatamab (approved May 2024). Immunotherapy rechallenge may be considered if not used in first-line. Clinical trial participation is strongly encouraged given limited efficacy of available options.
Original.Unnamed: 67: For 2nd-line ES-SCLC, current standard of care options include topotecan, lurbinectedin (approved 2020), or rechallenge with platinum-based chemotherapy. Tarlatamab (DLL3 bispecific) was recently approved in 2024 for relapsed ES-SCLC.
Original.Unnamed: 68: Platinum-based chemotherapy; trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) is approved for HER2-mutant NSCLC
Original.Unnamed: 69: For first-line EGFR-mutated NSCLC, the current standard of care is third-generation EGFR TKI monotherapy (osimertinib) or first/second-generation EGFR TKIs (erlotinib, gefitinib, afatinib)
Original.Unnamed: 70: For first-line treatment of HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer, the standard of care is the combination of taxane chemotherapy (e.g., paclitaxel or docetaxel), trastuzumab, and pertuzumab (THP regimen). However, recent data from DESTINY-Breast09 show that trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) plus pertuzumab is superior to THP and is likely to become the new standard of care.
Original.Unnamed: 71: Docetaxel chemotherapy for patients with advanced NSCLC who have progressed on anti-PD-(L)1 therapy and platinum-based chemotherapy
Original.Unnamed: 72: For metastatic NSCLC that has progressed on PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy, the current standard of care is typically docetaxel-based chemotherapy (with or without ramucirumab), pemetrexed (for non-squamous), or other platinum-based chemotherapy regimens. Clinical trial enrollment is also commonly considered at this stage.
Original.Unnamed: 73: For EGFR-mutated advanced NSCLC, current standard of care is first-line EGFR-TKI monotherapy, with osimertinib being the preferred third-generation TKI. FLAURA2 results support osimertinib plus chemotherapy as an emerging new standard.
Original.Unnamed: 74: For adjuvant treatment of ALK-positive NSCLC post-surgery, the current standard of care is adjuvant ALK inhibitor therapy (alectinib preferred based on ALINA trial results) following complete surgical resection. Adjuvant chemotherapy may also be considered. Observation was previously standard but is being replaced by targeted therapy.
Original.Unnamed: 75: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is the standard of care for most patients with clinically node-positive disease and for a large proportion of triple-negative and HER2-positive breast cancers. For high-risk, early-stage ER+/HER2- breast cancer, neoadjuvant chemotherapy is also standard, with pembrolizumab increasingly added for improved outcomes.
Original.Unnamed: 76: Surgery (breast-conserving surgery or mastectomy) is the standard initial treatment for DCIS. For most patients, this is followed by radiation therapy and/or hormone therapy (tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors for hormone receptor-positive cases). For low-risk DCIS, active surveillance is emerging as an alternative, but surgery remains the standard.

Original.Unnamed: 24: No direct competing trials for this biomarker-selected population
Original.Unnamed: 28: ADT intensification with abiraterone or other agents
Original.Unnamed: 30: Other AR inhibitor trials in biochemical recurrence
Original.Unnamed: 33: DESTINY-Breast06 (trastuzumab deruxtecan in HER2-low first-line), KATHERINE (trastuzumab emtansine adjuvant), other HER2-targeted ADCs in development for HER2-low disease
Original.Unnamed: 34: Alpelisib (SOLAR-1, NCT02437318), Capivasertib (CAPItello-291, NCT04862663), Elacestrant (EMERALD, NCT03778931), Other PI3K inhibitor trials in PIK3CA-mutated HR+/HER2- breast cancer
Original.Unnamed: 35: Other CD20×CD3 bispecifics (glofitamab, mosunetuzumab, odronextamab) in various FL trials, CAR-T therapies being evaluated in FL (axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel), tazemetostat for EZH2-mutant FL, PI3K inhibitors, and various combination immunotherapy trials in R/R FL
Original.Unnamed: 36: 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera - approved), everolimus, sunitinib. Competing trials: NETTER-2 (177Lu-DOTATATE in advanced GEP-NETs), COMPOSE trial (177Lu-edotreotide in aggressive Grade 2/3 GEP-NETs)
Original.Unnamed: 37: Durvalumab (POTOMAC trial NCT03732677 in MIBC), pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-866 NCT03924895 arm A), atezolizumab (IMvigor010 NCT02450331 adjuvant), nivolumab (CheckMate 274 NCT02632409 adjuvant)
Original.Unnamed: 38: DeLLphi-301 (tarlatamab Phase 2, NCT05060016), IDeate-Lung01 (ifinatamab deruxtecan DLL3-targeting ADC, NCT05280470), other DLL3-targeting bispecific and ADC programs in development for SCLC
Original.Unnamed: 39: Elacestrant (EMERALD trial, NCT03778931), Camizestrant (SERENA-4 trial, NCT04711252), Giredestrant (persevERA BC trial, NCT04546009), Amcenestrant (AMEERA trials)
Original.Unnamed: 40: Elacestrant (EMERALD trial, NCT03778931), Camizestrant (SERENA-4 trial), Imlunestrant (EMBER-3 trial), Lasofoxifene (ELAINE trials)
Original.Unnamed: 41: Key competing drugs include sunitinib (Sutent), everolimus (Afinitor), and lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (Lutathera). Notable ongoing trials include the Phase 3 SURUFATINIB trial (NCT02549937) evaluating surufatinib for advanced neuroendocrine tumors, and the Phase 3 NETTER-2 trial (NCT03972488) evaluating lutetium Lu 177 dotatate in earlier lines of therapy. Other trials include those evaluating lenvatinib and various chemotherapy combinations.
Original.Unnamed: 42: INAVO120, INAVO121, INAVO122 trials (inavolisib), EMBRACE (elacestrant), EMBER-3 (imlunestrant), PADA-1 (palbociclib + fulvestrant), PACE (palbociclib + fulvestrant), PENELOPE-B (palbociclib), NATALEE (ribociclib), monarchE (abemaciclib), CAPItello-291 (capivasertib), BYLieve (alpelisib), NCT04216472 (giredestrant), NCT04216472 (camizestrant), NCT04216472 (amcenestrant), NCT04216472 (AZD9833), NCT04216472 (AZD9496), NCT04216472 (onapristone), NCT04216472 (ZN-c3)
Original.Unnamed: 43: Key competing drugs: Teclistamab (Tecvayli), elranatamab (Elrexfio), talquetamab, cilta-cel (Carvykti), ide-cel (Abecma). Key competing trials: MajesTEC-1 (teclistamab monotherapy in RRMM), MagnetisMM-3 (elranatamab vs standard regimens in RRMM), MonumenTAL-1 (talquetamab in RRMM), CARTITUDE-4 (cilta-cel in earlier RRMM), KarMMa-3 (ide-cel in RRMM)
Original.Unnamed: 44: BRUIN CLL-313 (NCT05023980, pirtobrutinib vs bendamustine-rituximab in treatment-naïve CLL/SLL), BRUIN CLL-321 (NCT04666038, pirtobrutinib vs idelalisib-rituximab or bendamustine-rituximab in BTK inhibitor-pretreated), ELEVATE-TN (acalabrutinib trials), SEQUOIA (zanubrutinib trials)
Original.Unnamed: 45: Elacestrant (EMERALD trial - approved), Camizestrant (SERENA-4 trial), Imlunestrant (EMBER-3 trial)
Original.Unnamed: 46: BEACON CRC (encorafenib + cetuximab in later-line BRAF V600E mCRC), FOLFOXIRI-bevacizumab trials in first-line mCRC, and other trials evaluating BRAF/EGFR/MEK inhibitor combinations
Original.Unnamed: 47:
ADVANCE trial (NCT04268498) comparing DKRd vs KRd in newly diagnosed multiple myeloma
MANHATTAN trial (single-arm DKRd)
ENDURANCE trial comparing VRd vs KRd
IRAKLIA trial (NCT05405166) comparing subcutaneous vs intravenous isatuximab plus pomalidomide and dexamethasone in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma
IZALCO trial (NCT05704049) evaluating subcutaneous isatuximab plus carfilzomib and dexamethasone in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma
Original.Unnamed: 48: Other CD20xCD3 bispecifics in development for first-line DLBCL (glofitamab, epcoritamab, mosunetuzumab in various Phase 3 trials); CAR-T therapies being evaluated in earlier lines; polatuzumab vedotin combinations
Original.Unnamed: 49: Talazoparib (PARP inhibitor approved for metastatic setting); other adjuvant trials include CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib, ribociclib) in HR+ disease
Original.Unnamed: 50: Neoadjuvant immunotherapy approaches (NICHE trial); pembrolizumab in metastatic dMMR setting (KEYNOTE-177); other PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in development for adjuvant dMMR colon cancer
Original.Unnamed: 51: Pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-177, approved first-line); dostarlimab trials in MSI-H/dMMR CRC; other PD-1/PD-L1 combination trials in development
Original.Unnamed: 52: Key competing therapies include pembrolizumab + ipilimumab, nivolumab + ipilimumab, and relatlimab + nivolumab (Opdualag). Notable ongoing Phase 3 trials include NCT05539622 (tebentafusp), NCT04669051 (lifileucel), and NCT05217446 (bispecific antibodies targeting LAG-3/PD-1).
Original.Unnamed: 53: IMpassion031 (atezolizumab + chemotherapy in early-stage TNBC), ASCENT (sacituzumab govitecan in metastatic TNBC), CheckMate 7FL (nivolumab in early-stage TNBC)
Original.Unnamed: 54: Nivolumab + ipilimumab (CheckMate 142 and other trials), dostarlimab trials in MSI-H/dMMR CRC
Original.Unnamed: 55: POLARIX trial (polatuzumab vedotin + R-CHP vs R-CHOP, NCT03274492), ongoing trials of bispecific antibodies including glofitamab and epcoritamab for DLBCL, other investigational ADC combinations in development
Original.Unnamed: 56: Osimertinib (FLAURA trial, NCT02296125), other EGFR TKI trials in 1st-line EGFR+ NSCLC, MARIPOSA-2 (NCT04988295, post-osimertinib setting)
Original.Unnamed: 57: EV-302 (enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab for advanced urothelial cancer), KEYNOTE-905/EV-303 (pembrolizumab-based perioperative therapy), VOLGA (durvalumab + tremelimumab + enfortumab vedotin, NCT04960709), CheckMate 274 (nivolumab adjuvant therapy)
Original.Unnamed: 58: Pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-522 for neoadjuvant TNBC); other cancer vaccine trials in development; no direct competing adjuvant vaccine trials identified
Original.Unnamed: 59: Brexucabtagene autoleucel (approved CAR-T, NCT02601313), pirtobrutinib (BTK inhibitor, NCT04662255), venetoclax combinations in various trials, acalabrutinib + obinutuzumab + glofitamab (NCT06054776)
Original.Unnamed: 60: NCT02010606 (DCVax-L dendritic cell vaccine), NCT02209948 (phase II comparison of 6 vs 12 cycles of temozolomide), and various trials evaluating dose-dense temozolomide regimens and immunotherapies.
Original.Unnamed: 61: Major competing trials include monarchE (abemaciclib), NATALEE (ribociclib), and ongoing studies evaluating novel SERDs and CDK4/6 inhibitor combinations. Notable competing therapies are palbociclib and ribociclib in both metastatic and adjuvant settings.
Original.Unnamed: 62: OlympiAD (talazoparib in metastatic setting), EMBRACA (talazoparib Phase 3 in metastatic BRCA+ breast cancer)
Original.Unnamed: 63: Key competing drugs include sotorasib (Lumakras) and adagrasib (Krazati). Notable ongoing trials include CodeBreaK 200 (NCT04185883) for sotorasib and KRYSTAL-12 (NCT04685135) for adagrasib in similar patient populations.
Original.Unnamed: 64: TROPION-Lung01 (datopotamab deruxtecan, NCT05215340), MARIPOSA-2 (amivantamab + chemotherapy, NCT04988295), OptiTROP-Lung03 (sacituzumab tirumotecan vs docetaxel, NCT05631262)
Original.Unnamed: 65: Pembrolizumab (NICHE trial and other neoadjuvant studies in dMMR rectal cancer), nivolumab (various neoadjuvant immunotherapy trials), other PD-1 inhibitors in development for organ-sparing approaches in dMMR/MSI-H rectal cancer
Original.Unnamed: 66: Tarlatamab (DeLLphi-301, NCT05060016, approved May 2024), lurbinectedin + atezolizumab (IMforte, NCT05091567), datopotamab deruxtecan in SCLC trials, other B7-H3 and DLL3-targeted therapies in development
Original.Unnamed: 67: Key competing trials in relapsed ES-SCLC include trials of tarlatamab (DLL3 bispecific, now approved), ifinatamab deruxtecan (I-DXd, DLL3 ADC in IDEATE-LUNG01), and other DLL3-targeting bispecifics. Lurbinectedin is already approved as a competing option.
Original.Unnamed: 68: DESTINY-Lung02 (trastuzumab deruxtecan), other HER2-targeting trials in development
Original.Unnamed: 69: Osimertinib monotherapy (standard of care), amivantamab + osimertinib combinations (MARIPOSA trials), other EGFR TKI + chemotherapy combinations, EGFR x HER3 ADC trials in similar settings
Original.Unnamed: 70: DESTINY-Breast09 (NCT05113251), EmpowHER-303 (NCT05113251), and other ongoing trials evaluating T-DXd, zanidatamab, trastuzumab rezetecan, and zongertinib in HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer.
Original.Unnamed: 71: Other immunotherapy combinations in development for immunotherapy-refractory NSCLC; docetaxel remains standard comparator
Original.Unnamed: 72:
Key competing drugs: Docetaxel (standard comparator), ipilimumab (approved CTLA-4 inhibitor), other chemotherapy agents
Key competing trials: Other Phase 3 trials in post-immunotherapy NSCLC testing novel immunotherapy combinations, targeted therapies, and chemotherapy regimens
Original.Unnamed: 73: MARIPOSA trial (amivantamab + lazertinib vs osimertinib, NCT04487080); other EGFR-TKI combination trials; FLAURA trial (osimertinib monotherapy vs first-generation TKIs)
Original.Unnamed: 74: Key competing drugs: alectinib (superior efficacy in ALINA trial), brigatinib, lorlatinib. Key competing trials: ALINA (NCT03456076, alectinib vs chemotherapy - positive results), CROWN (NCT03052608, lorlatinib), ALTA-1L (NCT02737501, brigatinib)
Original.Unnamed: 75: KEYNOTE-522 (pembrolizumab + chemotherapy in early TNBC); Other investigational cancer vaccines for breast cancer prevention; Neoadjuvant immunotherapy combinations in TNBC
Original.Unnamed: 76:
LORD trial (NCT02492607, Europe): Active surveillance vs. surgery for low-risk DCIS
LORIS trial (ISRCTN27544579, UK): Active surveillance vs. surgery for low-risk DCIS
LORETTA trial (UMIN000028298, Japan): Single-arm study of tamoxifen for 5 years as alternative to surgery
RECAST DCIS (NCT06075953, US): Active surveillance with endocrine therapy vs. standard surgery
NRG CC-016: Using molecular testing to determine need for radiation after lumpectomy

Original.Unnamed: 70: The EmpowHER-303 trial (NCT06435429) is currently enrolling participants as of November 2025. The study is a global, Phase 3, open-label trial evaluating zanidatamab in combination with physician's choice chemotherapy versus trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer who have progressed on or are intolerant to prior trastuzumab deruxtecan treatment. The trial is being conducted at approximately 170 sites worldwide and aims to enroll about 550 participants.
Original.Unnamed: 71: Recruitment complete; estimated primary completion May 2025

Original.Unnamed: 74: Trial terminated in April 2025 following Data Safety Monitoring Committee recommendation. Enrollment completed August 2024 with 166 patients across 84 sites. Primary analysis showed adjuvant crizotinib did not improve DFS or OS compared to observation.
Original.Unnamed: 75: Phase 1 near completion as of November 2024, with 26 patients enrolled across three cohorts. Vaccine demonstrated safety and tolerability with injection site irritation as primary adverse event. Over 70% of patients exhibited protocol-defined immune responses. Final Phase 1 results to be presented at San Antonio Breast Cancer Symposium December 11, 2025. Phase 2 trial in neoadjuvant setting planned to commence in 2025, with IND sponsorship transferring from Cleveland Clinic to Anixa Biosciences.
Original.Unnamed: 76: Enrollment completed; 2-year primary analysis results published December 2024 in JAMA and presented at SABCS 2024; data lock June 30, 2024; currently in long-term follow-up phase with BCRF support

Original.Unnamed: 29: Median DFS not reached vs 86.1 months (HR 0.70; 95% CI 0.52-0.94; p=0.0155); 30% risk reduction; PC-specific DFS 38% risk reduction
Original.Unnamed: 33: Significant improvement in progression-free survival (median 10.1 vs 5.4 months in HR+ cohort, HR 0.51) and overall survival (median 23.9 vs 17.5 months, HR 0.64) compared to chemotherapy
Original.Unnamed: 34: Final analysis showed significant PFS improvement (median 15.0 vs 7.3 months, HR 0.43, p<0.001) and OS benefit (median 34.0 vs 27.0 months, HR 0.67, p=0.019); ORR 58.4% vs 25.0%
Original.Unnamed: 35: Preplanned interim analysis (announced August 2025) showed ORR 89% vs 74% (p<0.0001) and PFS HR 0.21 (95% CI: 0.13-0.33, p<0.0001), representing 79% reduction in risk of disease progression or death. Median PFS not reached in epcoritamab arm vs 11.2 months in control arm.
Original.Unnamed: 36: Median PFS 23.9 months vs 14.1 months with everolimus (p=0.022); ORR 21.9% vs 4.2% (p<0.0001); median OS 63.4 months vs 58.7 months (not statistically significant)
Original.Unnamed: 37: Interim analysis (announced August 2025) showed statistically significant improvement in event-free survival (primary endpoint), as well as improvements in overall survival and pathologic complete response rate; no new safety signals identified
Original.Unnamed: 38: Prespecified interim analysis (data cutoff January 29, 2025) showed significant OS benefit: median OS 13.6 months vs 8.3 months with chemotherapy (HR 0.60, P<0.001); median PFS 4.2 months vs 3.2 months (HR 0.72, P<0.001); ORR 35% vs 20%
Original.Unnamed: 39: No interim results publicly disclosed for this trial
Original.Unnamed: 40: Interim analysis showed statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in invasive disease-free survival versus standard-of-care endocrine therapy (specific HR and p-value not disclosed); overall survival data immature but showing positive trend
Original.Unnamed: 41: Interim analysis showed significant PFS benefit: Pancreatic NETs: median PFS 13.8 months vs 4.4 months placebo; Extra-pancreatic NETs: 8.4 months vs 3.9 months. The study met its primary endpoint and was stopped early for efficacy.
Original.Unnamed: 42: Interim analysis showed median PFS of 15.0 months with inavolisib + palbociclib + fulvestrant vs 7.3 months with placebo + palbociclib + fulvestrant; more than doubled progression-free survival
Original.Unnamed: 44: Topline results announced July 2025: Primary endpoint met with pirtobrutinib demonstrating ORR noninferiority vs ibrutinib (nominal P < .05 favoring pirtobrutinib in ITT population); PFS data immature but trending favorably for pirtobrutinib in treatment-naïve subset; safety profile consistent with prior trials
Original.Unnamed: 45: Primary analysis (not interim) showed giredestrant plus everolimus significantly reduced risk of progression or death by 44% in ITT population (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.44-0.71, p<0.0001) and 62% in ESR1-mutated population (HR 0.38, 95% CI 0.27-0.54, p<0.0001); median PFS 8.77 months vs 5.49 months in ITT; OS data immature but showing positive trend; safety profile consistent with known effects of individual agents
Original.Unnamed: 46: Significant improvement in PFS (HR 0.53, 95% CI 0.41-0.68, p<0.001) and interim OS (HR 0.47, 95% CI 0.32-0.69); ORR 60.9% vs 40.0% (p=0.0008)
Original.Unnamed: 48: No interim results publicly disclosed
Original.Unnamed: 49: Third interim analysis (6.1 years median follow-up) showed sustained benefit: invasive disease-free survival HR 0.65 (95% CI 0.53-0.78), distant disease-free survival HR 0.65 (95% CI 0.53-0.81), overall survival HR 0.72 (95% CI 0.56-0.93) with 28% reduction in risk of death
Original.Unnamed: 50: Significant improvement in disease-free survival (HR 0.50; 3-year DFS 86.4% vs 76.6%; p<0.0001)
Original.Unnamed: 51: Primary endpoint met: Median PFS not reached with nivo+ipi vs 39.3 months with nivo alone (HR 0.62, 95% CI 0.48-0.81, p=0.0003) across all lines; median PFS 54.1 months vs 5.9 months vs chemotherapy in first-line (HR 0.21); ORR 71% vs 58% with nivo alone; results published in The Lancet January 2025 and presented at ASCO GI 2025
Original.Unnamed: 53: Final analysis showed pathologic complete response rate of 64.8% in pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy arm vs 51.2% in placebo plus chemotherapy arm (p<0.001); overall survival showed significant improvement with HR 0.63 (95% CI 0.48-0.82)
Original.Unnamed: 54: Significant improvement in PFS (HR 0.60, 95% CI 0.45-0.80, P=0.0004); OS data immature at interim analysis
Original.Unnamed: 56: Primary PFS analysis (June 2024) showed significant benefit: median PFS 23.7 vs 16.6 months (HR 0.70, P<0.001); OS data immature at that time
Original.Unnamed: 57: Positive interim analysis (2024): Median EFS not reached in durvalumab arm vs 46.1 months in chemotherapy arm (HR 0.68, 95% CI 0.56-0.82, p<0.0001); Median OS not reached in either arm (HR 0.75, 95% CI 0.59-0.93, p=0.0106); pCR rate 33.8% vs 25.8% (OR 1.49, p=0.0038)
Original.Unnamed: 58: Phase 1 results presented at SITC 2024: vaccine demonstrated safety and tolerability across all three cohorts; strong immune responses observed in Cohort 1a; similar safety profile in Cohort 1b with ongoing analysis; no major adverse events in Cohort 1c (vaccine + pembrolizumab) with confirmed immune responses
Original.Unnamed: 60: No interim results publicly disclosed. Trial was suspended before completion.
Original.Unnamed: 61: Interim analysis showed a 38% reduction in progression or death with imlunestrant monotherapy versus standard endocrine therapy in patients with ESR1 mutations, and a 43% reduction with imlunestrant plus abemaciclib versus imlunestrant monotherapy in all patients regardless of ESR1 mutation status. Median PFS was 9.4 months for the combination and 5.5 months for monotherapy.
Original.Unnamed: 62: First interim analysis (2.5 years median follow-up): 42% reduction in invasive disease or death (HR 0.58, p<0.001); 3-year iDFS 85.9% vs 77.1%. Second interim analysis (3.5 years): 32% reduction in death (HR 0.68, p=0.009); 4-year OS 89.8% vs 86.4%
Original.Unnamed: 63: Phase 1 results published April 2025: ORR 73.5% (25/34) in G12Ci-naive patients; 66.7% in NSCLC, 88.9% in CRC, 75.0% in PDAC. Grade 3 TRAEs in 16.7% of patients; no grade 4/5 TRAEs. D3S-001 600 mg selected for further investigation.
Original.Unnamed: 64: No interim results publicly disclosed for NCT06132958; however, related Phase 2 trials (OptiTROP-Lung01, NCT05351788 and OptiTROP-Lung03, NCT05631262) showed promising efficacy with ORR of 45-78% and manageable safety profile
Original.Unnamed: 65: No interim results from AZUR-1 reported as of November 2025. Trial initiated based on predecessor phase 2 study (NCT04165772) showing 100% clinical complete response in 42 patients with median follow-up of 17.9 months.
Original.Unnamed: 66: No interim results publicly disclosed for Phase 3 IDeate-Lung02 trial
Original.Unnamed: 69: None reported for Phase 3 trial; Phase 2 data (NCT05880706) showed 100% ORR, 95% confirmed ORR, 92.1% 12-month PFS rate
Original.Unnamed: 70: No interim analysis results have been publicly disclosed for the EmpowHER-303 trial as of November 2025.
Original.Unnamed: 71: No interim results publicly disclosed
Original.Unnamed: 72: No interim efficacy results publicly disclosed; independent data monitoring committee assessment in October 2024 identified variance in results between squamous and non-squamous NSCLC populations, leading to trial modification
Original.Unnamed: 73: Initial analysis (2023) showed significant PFS benefit (HR 0.62, 95% CI 0.49-0.79, p<0.001); median PFS 25.5 months vs 16.7 months. OS data immature at initial analysis. Final OS analysis (2025) showed median OS 47.5 months vs 37.6 months (HR 0.77, p=0.02).
Original.Unnamed: 74: Final analysis results disclosed (trial terminated April 2025): Adjuvant crizotinib did not improve DFS (HR not significantly different from 1.0) or OS compared to observation. Grade 3+ treatment-related adverse events occurred in 43% of crizotinib patients. Treatment discontinuation due to adverse effects occurred in 27% of patients.
Original.Unnamed: 75: Safe and well-tolerated across all three cohorts. Primary adverse event was injection site irritation; no flu-like symptoms, abnormal laboratory tests, or major systemic adverse events observed, including in combination with pembrolizumab. Over 70% of patients exhibited protocol-defined immune responses with α-lactalbumin-specific T cell induced interferon gamma and interleukin-17. Maximum tolerated dose successfully identified.
Original.Unnamed: 76: 2-year primary analysis results published December 2024: 2-year ipsilateral invasive cancer rate was 5.9% (95% CI, 3.71-8.04) in guideline-concordant care arm vs 4.2% (95% CI, 2.31-6.00) in active monitoring arm (difference 1.7%), demonstrating noninferiority. No significant differences in invasive tumor size, node status, or tumor grade between groups. Quality of life remained stable and similar between arms.

Original.Unnamed: 15: ATR previously unsuccessful as NSCLC target
Original.Unnamed: 16: Toxicity profile may overshadow efficacy; promise in PD-L1-low/cold tumors
Original.Unnamed: 20: Similar toxicity to individual drugs; 6.8% discontinued due to AEs
Original.Unnamed: 23: Grade 3-4 treatment-related AEs: 72.3% combo vs 59.2% chemo
Original.Unnamed: 24: Only 3 severe aspirin-related AEs: GI bleeding, hematoma, allergic reaction
Original.Unnamed: 27: No new safety signals beyond pembrolizumab; well-tolerated
Original.Unnamed: 28: HRR mutations associated with worse survival (HR 1.6)
Original.Unnamed: 29: Common AEs: chills (33.4% vs 8.6%), flu-like symptoms (30.5% vs 13.8%), fever (25.1% vs 3.9%); mostly grade 1-2, self-limited
Original.Unnamed: 30: None reported; darolutamide structurally different for differentiated tolerability
Original.Unnamed: 33: Interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis observed in 12.1% of T-DXd patients (0.8% grade 5); manageable safety profile overall with no unexpected safety signals
Original.Unnamed: 34: Manageable safety profile with higher rates of hyperglycemia, stomatitis, and GI toxicity vs placebo; low discontinuation rate (6.8% vs 0.6%); no new safety signals with longer follow-up
Original.Unnamed: 35: Safety profile consistent with known profiles of individual agents. CRS occurred in 24% of patients (primarily low grade: 19% Grade 1, 5% Grade 2). Single ICANS event (0.8%, Grade 1). Boxed warnings for CRS and ICANS. No new safety signals identified.
Original.Unnamed: 36: 177Lu-edotreotide was well-tolerated with no unforeseen treatment-emergent adverse events; one grade 2 serious TEAE of myelodysplastic syndrome reported; lower discontinuation rate due to TEAEs (1.8%) vs everolimus (15.2%)
Original.Unnamed: 37: No new safety signals identified in interim analysis; known toxicities include dermatologic events (enfortumab vedotin-related) and immune-related adverse events (pembrolizumab-related)
Original.Unnamed: 38: Boxed Warning for life-threatening or fatal cytokine release syndrome and neurologic toxicity, including immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS); warnings and precautions for cytopenias, infections, hepatotoxicity, hypersensitivity, and embryo-fetal toxicity
Original.Unnamed: 40: No unexpected safety findings; adverse events consistent with known safety profile; well tolerated
Original.Unnamed: 41: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported; the safety profile was manageable and consistent with previous cabozantinib studies.
Original.Unnamed: 42: Grade 3/4 hyperglycemia, stomatitis, diarrhea, and ocular toxic effects (dry eye, blurred vision) reported; no new safety signals or protocol modifications disclosed
Original.Unnamed: 43: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported
Original.Unnamed: 44: No significant safety signals reported; safety profile consistent with prior pirtobrutinib trials
Original.Unnamed: 45: No new safety signals reported; adverse events consistent with known safety profiles of individual agents; most common AEs were stomatitis (47%), diarrhea (27%), and anemia (24%); grade 1/2 bradycardia observed in 3.8% of giredestrant arm vs 0.5% in control arm; no cases of photopsia; overall toxicity balanced between arms
Original.Unnamed: 46: No significant safety signals reported; safety profiles consistent with known profiles of each agent
Original.Unnamed: 48: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported
Original.Unnamed: 49: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported; adverse events consistent with known olaparib safety profile; low incidence of MDS/AML (4 olaparib vs 6 placebo at 6-year follow-up)
Original.Unnamed: 50: Manageable safety profile; treatment-related grade ≥3 AEs in 71.7% (atezolizumab arm) vs 62.1% (mFOLFOX6 alone); increased nonfebrile neutropenia; no protocol modifications reported
Original.Unnamed: 51: No new safety signals identified; safety profile consistent with known checkpoint inhibitor toxicities; any-grade TRAEs: 81% (nivo+ipi) vs 71% (nivo alone); most common: pruritus (26%), diarrhea (20%), hypothyroidism (17%); grade 3+ TRAEs: 23% vs 17%; manageable with established protocols
Original.Unnamed: 53: No major protocol modifications reported; safety profile consistent with known profiles of pembrolizumab and chemotherapy components; most common grade 3+ adverse events were neutropenia (34.5%), decreased neutrophil count (18.6%), anemia (18.0%)
Original.Unnamed: 54: No significant safety signals; pembrolizumab had fewer treatment-related adverse events than chemotherapy (grade 3-5: 21.6% vs 67.1%)
Original.Unnamed: 56: Prophylactic anticoagulation recommended during first 4 months of treatment to reduce venous thromboembolic event (VTE) risk; no major protocol modifications reported
Original.Unnamed: 57: No new safety signals; safety profile consistent with known durvalumab and platinum-based chemotherapy adverse events; no increased discontinuation rates or impact on surgery completion
Original.Unnamed: 58: No major adverse events reported across all three cohorts; vaccine demonstrated favorable safety profile; no dose-limiting toxicities identified; combination with pembrolizumab (Cohort 1c) showed no additional safety concerns
Original.Unnamed: 60: No significant safety signals reported. Trial suspended due to funding constraints, not safety concerns.
Original.Unnamed: 61: No significant safety signals reported; the safety profile of imlunestrant was favorable, without the oral SERD-specific safety signals of ocular or cardiac issues. The combination with abemaciclib had a safety profile consistent with known fulvestrant-abemaciclib, with a low discontinuation rate of 6.3%. No protocol modifications were reported.
Original.Unnamed: 62: Protocol amended in 2015 to expand from triple-negative only to include hormone receptor-positive disease. Safety profile favorable with low incidence of AML/MDS (0.2% olaparib vs 0.3% placebo at 3.5 years); no new safety signals identified
Original.Unnamed: 63: No dose-limiting toxicities or grade 4/5 treatment-related adverse events. Grade 3 TRAEs in 16.7% of G12Ci-naive patients. Dose modifications in 28.6% of patients due to TRAEs; no discontinuations. D3S-001 600 mg selected as recommended dose based on pharmacokinetics.
Original.Unnamed: 64: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported; Phase 2 data showed manageable safety profile with most common grade ≥3 AEs being neutropenia (30%), anemia (16%), and rash (5-6%); no treatment-related deaths
Original.Unnamed: 65: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported. Predecessor phase 2 study showed favorable safety profile with no grade 3 or higher adverse events.
Original.Unnamed: 66: None reported for Phase 3 trial
Original.Unnamed: 69: None reported for Phase 3 trial; Phase 1/2 data showed manageable hematologic toxicities (anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia) with low discontinuation rate (2.7%)
Original.Unnamed: 70: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications have been reported for the EmpowHER-303 trial as of November 2025.
Original.Unnamed: 71: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported
Original.Unnamed: 72: Partial clinical hold placed October 2024 due to varying results between squamous and non-squamous NSCLC populations; protocol modified December 2024 to restrict enrollment to squamous NSCLC only. Previous Phase 1/2 studies noted toxicity concerns at higher doses.
Original.Unnamed: 73: Grade ≥3 adverse events higher with combination (64% vs 27%), primarily hematologic and chemotherapy-related. Most common grade ≥3 AEs: neutropenia (30%), anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (9%). Safety profile manageable; no new safety signals identified. Discontinuation due to AEs: 12% (osimertinib) and 50% (pemetrexed) in combination arm.
Original.Unnamed: 74: Significant toxicity observed: 43% of patients experienced grade 3 or higher treatment-related adverse events. Most common grade 3+ events included diarrhea (8%), edema (5%), eye disorders, abdominal pain, elevated ALT, and decreased neutrophil count (3% each). One grade 4 stroke reported. Treatment discontinuation due to adverse effects occurred in 27% of patients.
Original.Unnamed: 75: Mild irritation at injection site; no serious side effects
Original.Unnamed: 76: No significant safety signals or protocol modifications reported. Trial design allowed for patient crossover between arms (anticipated in statistical analysis plan).

Original.Unnamed: 11: High recurrence rates after surgery justify ICI trials
Original.Unnamed: 13: Phase 2: Promising efficacy in R/R NPM1-mutant AML; company has $609.7M cash to support through pivotal results
Original.Unnamed: 14: Phase 1/2: 59.3% ORR in progressive disease, 59.0% in treatment-naive patients; manageable safety
Original.Unnamed: 15: Previous ATR trials in NSCLC unsuccessful; limited clinical data
Original.Unnamed: 16: Shows activity in PD-L1-low or "cold" tumors; concern about CTLA-4 toxicity
Original.Unnamed: 17: Phase 2 (S1800A): 136 patients showed extended survival vs standard treatment
Original.Unnamed: 18: Demonstrated significant PFS and OS improvement; marks practice-changing data in HER2-low space
Original.Unnamed: 19: Antitumor activity in HER2+ and HER2-low models; manageable safety; expanded therapeutic window
Original.Unnamed: 20: First treatment to show OS improvement with PIK3CA pathway targeting; MSK led Phase 1 trial
Original.Unnamed: 21: More potent than T-DM1 in preclinical HER2+ and HER2-low models; no activity in cardiomyocytes
Original.Unnamed: 22: Prior approval for 2nd-line BRAF V600E mCRC; historically limited 1st-line efficacy for this mutation
Original.Unnamed: 23: ICIs beneficial in metastatic dMMR CRC; unclear if benefit extends to adjuvant setting
Original.Unnamed: 24: Rationale based on PI3K pathway role in CRC; aspirin well-established cardiovascular drug
Original.Unnamed: 25: Phase 2: 67% ORR with lifileucel + pembrolizumab; durable responses in ~20 patients treated
Original.Unnamed: 27: Phase 1/2: 25.5-month median PFS with combination; no added systemic toxicity vs anti-PD-1 monotherapy
Original.Unnamed: 28: MAGNITUDE trial (mCRPC): BRCA+ patients showed 47% rPFS risk reduction with Akeega
Original.Unnamed: 29: 3 intraprostatic injections with favorable tolerability; targets unmet need in localized disease
Original.Unnamed: 30: ARAMIS trial (nmCRPC): Darolutamide significantly improved mFS and OS
Original.Unnamed: 31: Established efficacy leading to FDA approval for maintenance indication
Original.Unnamed: 33: Phase 1/2 studies showed promising efficacy in HER2-low metastatic breast cancer with ORR of 37.0-37.5% and median PFS of 6.3-11.1 months in heavily pretreated patients, supporting Phase 3 development
Original.Unnamed: 34: Phase 1/2 showed significant improvement in PFS
Original.Unnamed: 35: Phase 1/2 showed 82% ORR with 60% CR in heavily pretreated R/R FL, with manageable safety profile and primarily low-grade CRS and ICANS events
Original.Unnamed: 36: Positive Phase 2 retrospective study results supported Phase 3 initiation and led to FDA Fast Track Designation
Original.Unnamed: 37: Phase 1b/2 EV-103 trial showed high response rates (ORR 73.3%, CR 15.6%) and manageable safety with enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab in first-line metastatic urothelial carcinoma; Phase 3 EV-302 trial demonstrated significant survival benefit (HR 0.47 for OS) in first-line metastatic disease, supporting investigation in earlier-stage MIBC
Original.Unnamed: 38: Phase 1 (DeLLphi-300): ORR 23.4%, median DOR 12.3 months, median OS 13.2 months in heavily pretreated SCLC; manageable safety profile with 31% grade ≥3 TRAEs. Phase 2 (DeLLphi-301): ORR 40% (10 mg dose) with durable responses; led to FDA accelerated approval May 2024.
Original.Unnamed: 39: Phase 1b/2 EMBER study: Imlunestrant demonstrated manageable safety profile and preliminary antitumor activity in ER+/HER2- advanced breast cancer, with median PFS of 7.2 months at 400 mg dose in heavily pretreated patients (92% prior CDK4/6i exposure). Recommended phase 2 dose established at 400 mg once daily.
Original.Unnamed: 40: Phase 2 coopERA trial (neoadjuvant setting) showed giredestrant was superior to aromatase inhibitor in reducing malignant cell division (Ki67 levels) in ER+ HER2- early breast cancer, supporting advancement to Phase 3 adjuvant trial
Original.Unnamed: 41: Phase 1/2 studies demonstrated manageable safety and promising efficacy, supporting the initiation of the phase 3 CABINET trial. Early data showed significant delays in disease progression and favorable safety profile, leading to the decision to advance to phase 3.
Original.Unnamed: 42: Phase 1b/2 results demonstrated a manageable safety profile and promising efficacy, with median progression-free survival (PFS) of 15.0 months for inavolisib + palbociclib + fulvestrant versus 7.3 months for placebo + palbociclib + fulvestrant, more than doubling PFS. Grade 3/4 hyperglycemia, stomatitis, diarrhea, and ocular toxic effects were reported, but no new safety signals or protocol modifications were disclosed.
Original.Unnamed: 43: Phase 1/2 LINKER-MM1 showed 71% ORR at 200mg dose with manageable safety profile; 59% achieved VGPR or better, 30% achieved CR or better; CRS mostly Grade 1 (35%), with 9.4% Grade 2 and single Grade 3 event
Original.Unnamed: 44: Phase 1/2 showed 73% ORR in 317 patients with CLL/SLL
Original.Unnamed: 45: Phase 1/2 data demonstrated acceptable safety profile and preliminary efficacy signals supporting advancement to Phase 3; specific Phase 2 results not publicly detailed in available sources
Original.Unnamed: 46: BEACON CRC trial demonstrated significant benefit in later-line setting, with improved response rates and survival in patients with BRAF V600E-mutated metastatic colorectal cancer.
Original.Unnamed: 47: Phase 1/2 trials demonstrated a median overall survival of 21.7 months in nonrecurrent H3K27M-mutant diffuse midline glioma patients, with a favorable safety profile and evidence of durable responses.
Original.Unnamed: 48: Phase 2 ELM-2 showed 52% ORR and 31.5% CR in heavily pretreated R/R DLBCL
Original.Unnamed: 49: Phase 2 studies demonstrated PARP inhibitor efficacy in BRCA-mutant cancers with manageable safety profile; olaparib showed activity in metastatic BRCA-mutant breast cancer (OlympiAD trial), supporting evaluation in adjuvant setting
Original.Unnamed: 50: Phase 2 data supported efficacy in dMMR/MSI-H colorectal cancer; immune checkpoint inhibitors showed benefit in metastatic dMMR setting, supporting adjuvant evaluation
Original.Unnamed: 51: CheckMate-142 (Phase 2) demonstrated durable responses and manageable safety with nivolumab + ipilimumab in previously treated MSI-H/dMMR mCRC, with ORR of 55% and 12-month PFS of 71%, supporting initiation of CheckMate-8HW for first-line evaluation
Original.Unnamed: 52: Phase 1 data showed 57% ORR in treatment-naive patients and 46% ORR in those previously treated with anti-PD-1, with 25-31% complete response rates and an acceptable safety profile.
Original.Unnamed: 53: Phase 2 showed significant improvement in pathologic complete response
Original.Unnamed: 54: Phase 2 KEYNOTE-016 showed durable responses with pembrolizumab in previously treated MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors including CRC, supporting Phase 3 development in first-line setting
Original.Unnamed: 55: Phase 2 waveLINE-007 (NCT05406401) showed 100% complete response rate at recommended Phase 2 dose of 1.75 mg/kg zilovertamab vedotin plus R-CHP in previously untreated DLBCL (presented ASH 2024)
Original.Unnamed: 56: Phase 1b CHRYSALIS study: Amivantamab + lazertinib demonstrated antitumor activity in treatment-naive EGFR+ NSCLC with manageable safety profile, supporting Phase 3 advancement
Original.Unnamed: 57: ABACUS (Phase 2) showed benefit of neoadjuvant durvalumab monotherapy in MIBC, with manageable safety profile, supporting Phase 3 NIAGARA trial design combining durvalumab with chemotherapy
Original.Unnamed: 58: Preclinical studies in mice showed safe and effective prevention of breast tumors through immune system activation against α-lactalbumin; provided rationale for Phase 1 human trial
Original.Unnamed: 59: Phase 1/2 (NCT05471843) met primary endpoint of ORR in heavily pretreated R/R MCL patients post-BTK inhibitor and anti-CD20 therapy, showing high response rates including complete responses, with manageable safety profile and no dose-limiting toxicities; results supported FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation in October 2025
Original.Unnamed: 60: Phase 1 data showed 55% three-year overall survival (vs 16% historical SOC), 38% disease-free at 3 years (vs 6% historical), median OS 38.4 months (vs 14.6 months historical), median PFS 17 months (vs 6.9 months historical), and 12-month disease-free survival 75% (vs 26.9% historical). No serious adverse events were reported, with minor side effects limited to fatigue, skin rash, and pruritis. Phase 2 randomized trial (124 patients) showed PFS improvement of 2.2 months (p=0.011) though OS primary endpoint was not met; vaccine was well tolerated with maintained quality of life.
Original.Unnamed: 61: Phase 2: ORR 45%, median PFS 8.2 months in pretreated NSCLC (published Lancet Oncology 2024); Phase 1b/2: Manageable safety profile, ORR 38% in SCLC (ASCO 2023); Phase 2: Superior PFS vs. chemotherapy (HR 0.55) in biomarker-selected population
Original.Unnamed: 62: Phase 2 metastatic breast cancer (OlympiAD): Olaparib showed significant PFS benefit vs chemotherapy in BRCA-mutated metastatic breast cancer (median PFS 7.0 vs 4.2 months, HR 0.58), with manageable safety profile, supporting Phase 3 adjuvant study
Original.Unnamed: 63: Phase 1 results (published April 2025): ORR 73.5% (25/34) in G12Ci-naive patients overall; 66.7% in NSCLC (14/21), 88.9% in CRC (8/9), 75.0% in PDAC (3/4). Favorable safety profile with no grade 4/5 treatment-related adverse events. Grade 3 TRAEs in 16.7% of patients. D3S-001 600 mg selected for Phase 2 expansion based on pharmacokinetics and efficacy.
Original.Unnamed: 64: Phase 1/2 data (OptiTROP-Lung01, NCT05351788) in EGFR-mutated NSCLC showed ORR of 48.6-77.6%, DCR of 94.6-100%, and median PFS of 15.4 months with manageable safety profile; Phase 2 data (OptiTROP-Lung03, NCT05631262) showed ORR of 45.1% vs 15.6% with docetaxel, supporting Phase 3 advancement
Original.Unnamed: 65: Phase 2 (NCT04165772): 100% clinical complete response (cCR) in all 42 patients who completed 6 months of dostarlimab treatment; sustained cCR with median follow-up of 26.3 months in first 24 patients; no grade 3 or higher adverse events reported; results published in NEJM 2022 and led to FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation
Original.Unnamed: 66: Phase 2 IDeate-Lung01 (NCT05280470): ORR 48.2% (95% CI 39.6-56.9%) at 12 mg/kg dose, median DOR 5.3 months, DCR 87.6%, median TTR 1.4 months (presented at WCLC September 2025). Phase 1/2 IDeate-PanTumor01 (NCT04145622): ORR 52.4%, median DOR 5.9 months, median OS 12.2 months in 21 ES-SCLC patients. Manageable safety profile supported Phase 3 advancement.
Original.Unnamed: 67: Phase 2 (TROPiCS-03): ORR 41.9% (95% CI, 27.0%-57.9%), median PFS 4.4 months, median OS 13.6 months in 2nd-line ES-SCLC; manageable safety profile consistent with prior studies (IASLC 2024)
Original.Unnamed: 68: Phase I/II SOHO-01 study demonstrated promising safety and efficacy as a second-line therapy in patients with advanced NSCLC and HER2 mutations, supporting progression to Phase III
Original.Unnamed: 69: Phase 2 combination data (iza-bren + osimertinib): 100% ORR, 95% confirmed ORR, 92.1% 12-month PFS rate, manageable hematologic toxicities (NCT05880706, presented WCLC 2025)
Original.Unnamed: 70: Phase 2: In the DESTINY-Breast03 trial, trastuzumab deruxtecan demonstrated a median progression-free survival (PFS) of 25.1 months versus 5.4 months with trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1), with an objective response rate (ORR) of 79.7% versus 34.2%. The safety profile was manageable, with the most common grade 3 or higher adverse events being neutropenia (12%) and interstitial lung disease (ILD) (10%). These results led to the initiation of Phase 3 trials for further evaluation in earlier lines of therapy.
Original.Unnamed: 71: Phase 2 HUDSON study: ORR 13.9%, median PFS 6.0 months, median OS 15.9 months in immunotherapy-refractory NSCLC; manageable safety profile supported Phase 3 advancement
Original.Unnamed: 72: Phase 1/2 results showed some activity in low TPS and STK-11/KEAP-1 mutated tumors, but with toxicity concerns
Original.Unnamed: 73: Phase 1/2 data for osimertinib plus chemotherapy combination not specifically detailed in available sources. FLAURA2 was designed based on rationale that combination therapy might overcome resistance mechanisms and improve outcomes beyond osimertinib monotherapy.
Original.Unnamed: 74: Phase 1/2 results demonstrated high response rates (approximately 60% ORR) and manageable safety profile in ALK-positive NSCLC, with progression-free survival of 7-10 months in metastatic setting, supporting progression to Phase 3 adjuvant trial.
Original.Unnamed: 75: Preclinical research by Dr. Vincent Tuohy at Cleveland Clinic showed that activating the immune system against α-lactalbumin was safe and effective in preventing breast tumors in mice. The vaccine targets a lactation protein that is re-expressed in many breast cancers, providing rationale for immunization as a preventive and therapeutic approach.
Original.Unnamed: 76: N/A - Phase 3 trial designed based on clinical rationale and observational data about DCIS overtreatment, not based on previous phase drug development results

Validated.Unnamed: 73: Adults with previously untreated, locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) harboring EGFR mutations, specifically those with somatic activating sensitizing mutations such as exon 19 deletions or exon 21 L858R, and not eligible if they have non-sensitizing mutations (e.g., EGFR exon 20 insertions). Patients must have advanced disease (stage IIIB, IIIC, or IV) per AJCC/IASLC 8th edition criteria.
Validated.Unnamed: 74: Patients with completely resected stage IB (tumors ≥4 cm), II, or IIIA non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with ALK rearrangement, who have not received previous systemic anticancer therapy.
Validated.Unnamed: 75: Three cohorts: 1a) Patients who completed treatment for early-stage triple-negative breast cancer within the past 3 years, currently tumor-free but at high risk for recurrence; 1b) Cancer-free individuals at high risk for developing breast cancer (primarily BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2 mutations) who have elected preventive mastectomy; 1c) Patients with early-stage triple-negative breast cancer with residual disease after neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy and surgery, receiving pembrolizumab post-surgery
Validated.Unnamed: 76: Female patients aged 40 years or older with newly diagnosed, screen-detected, grade 1 or 2, hormone receptor-positive (estrogen and/or progesterone receptor ≥10% staining), HER2-negative ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) without evidence of invasive or microinvasive disease. Patients with breast symptoms or mass on baseline imaging were excluded. Concurrence of 2 pathologists required to confirm eligibility.
